  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2021.0070; PMID 33625512
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33625512/
  • Date: 2021 Feb 1
  • Category: Side Effects & Quality of Life; Guidelines & Care Pathways
  • Evidence type: Cross-sectional health-system analysis.
  • Population: 90,494 US veterans with prostate cancer; includes men initiating ADT and men with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
  • Patient relevance: Shows real-world gaps/opportunities in BP, cholesterol, and glucose assessment and management around prostate cancer care.
  • Main finding: Cardiovascular risk factor assessment and control are central survivorship issues, especially for men initiating ADT.
  • Limitations: Veterans Health Administration population; observational health-system data.
  • Questions for clinician: Have BP, LDL/cholesterol, glucose/HbA1c, smoking, weight, and exercise been explicitly reviewed before ADT?

docs.title: Treatment-related blood pressure and cardiovascular risk
Quantifying risk of metabolic syndrome and components following ADT: meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Bosco C, et al. PLOS ONE. 2015.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0117344; PMID 25794005
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25794005/
  • Date: 2015
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis.
  • Population: Nine studies of men receiving ADT.
  • Patient relevance: ADT is common in advanced/high-risk prostate cancer and can worsen metabolic risk.
  • Main finding: ADT was associated with increased metabolic syndrome risk; diabetes risk was also increased. Hypertension-specific evidence was less robust but part of the cardiometabolic risk frame.
  • Limitations: Limited number of studies for individual components; observational designs included.
  • Questions for clinician: What cardiometabolic monitoring schedule is needed when starting ADT?

docs.title: Pharmacologic androgen deprivation and cardiovascular disease risk factors: systematic review
docs.text:
• Citation: European Journal of Clinical Investigation. 2015.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/eci.12431; PMID 25753698
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25753698/
  • Date: 2015 May
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of randomized controlled trials reporting cardiovascular risk factors.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer in RCTs comparing pharmacologic ADT with no ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Looks at BP and other cardiovascular risk factors in a trial context.
  • Main finding: ADT affects cardiovascular risk factors; BP reporting was less consistent than other metabolic outcomes.
  • Limitations: Risk-factor outcomes were not always primary endpoints; trial populations may underrepresent frail patients or those with major comorbidities.
  • Questions for clinician: Which risk factors are most likely to worsen for me: BP, weight, glucose, lipids, or body composition?

docs.title: Association of ADT with metabolic disease in prostate cancer patients: updated meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Clinical Genitourinary Cancer. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.clgc.2022.12.006; PMID 36621463
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36621463/
  • Date: 2023 Jun
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Updated meta-analysis.
  • Population: 19 studies of ADT-associated metabolic outcomes.
  • Patient relevance: Quantifies metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and hypertension domains in ADT-treated men.
  • Main finding: ADT was associated with higher metabolic syndrome risk and diabetes risk; hypertension is part of the monitored metabolic disease burden.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneous study designs, controls, and definitions.
  • Questions for clinician: Should ADT care include baseline and follow-up BP, glucose/HbA1c, lipids, weight, and exercise/nutrition planning?

docs.title: Cardiovascular Events and Androgen Receptor Signaling Inhibitors in Advanced Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: JAMA Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamaoncol.2024.1549; PMID 38842801
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38842801/
  • Date: 2024 Jul 1
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
  • Population: Men with locally advanced or metastatic prostate cancer treated with androgen receptor signaling inhibitors added to standard care.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant when considering abiraterone, enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide-class intensification.
  • Main finding: AR signaling inhibitors improve prostate cancer outcomes in appropriate settings but add cardiovascular toxicity concerns, including hypertension-related monitoring needs.
  • Limitations: Trial populations may exclude high-risk cardiovascular patients; adverse event reporting varies.
  • Questions for clinician: What is the benefit of treatment intensification, and how will BP/cardiovascular toxicity be monitored and managed?

docs.title: Cardiovascular events among men treated with androgen receptor pathway inhibitors
docs.text:
• Citation: Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/s41391-024-00886-0; PMID 39237679
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39237679/
  • Date: 2025 Jun
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review, meta-analysis, and network meta-analysis of RCTs.
  • Population: 26 RCTs of abiraterone, apalutamide, darolutamide, and enzalutamide.
  • Patient relevance: Helps compare cardiovascular toxicity across AR pathway inhibitors.
  • Main finding: ARPIs were associated with increased cardiac disorders, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and hypertension-related cardiovascular concern signals.
  • Limitations: Indirect comparisons, heterogeneous disease states and treatment durations.
  • Questions for clinician: If several ARPI options are reasonable, should cardiovascular history influence drug choice?

docs.title: Risk of hypertension in cancer patients treated with abiraterone: meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Clinical Hypertension. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1186/s40885-019-0110-3; PMID 30984412
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30984412/
  • Date: 2019
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
  • Population: Five trials, 5,445 patients treated with abiraterone plus prednisone/prednisolone versus controls.
  • Patient relevance: Specific to a common advanced prostate cancer therapy.
  • Main finding: Abiraterone was associated with significantly increased all-grade and high-grade hypertension risk.
  • Limitations: Trial selection and adverse-event reporting; BP management protocols vary.
  • Questions for clinician: If abiraterone is considered, how will BP, potassium, edema/fluid retention, and heart failure risk be monitored?

docs.title: Increased Risk of Hypertension with Enzalutamide in Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Cancer Investigation. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1080/07357907.2019.1670203; PMID 31557062
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31557062/
  • Date: 2019
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials.
  • Population: Seven studies, 7,347 patients treated with enzalutamide.
  • Patient relevance: Specific to enzalutamide, used in several prostate cancer disease states.
  • Main finding: Enzalutamide was associated with increased all-grade and high-grade hypertension risk.
  • Limitations: Trial populations and adverse event definitions vary.
  • Questions for clinician: If enzalutamide is considered, how often should BP be checked and what threshold triggers treatment adjustment?

docs.title: Risk of cardiovascular toxicities and hypertension in nmCRPC treated with novel hormonal agents
docs.text:
• Citation: Expert Opinion on Drug Metabolism & Toxicology. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1080/17425255.2021.1970745; PMID 34407702
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34407702/
  • Date: 2021 Oct
  • Category: Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs.
  • Population: 4,110 men with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer treated with enzalutamide, apalutamide, or darolutamide plus ADT versus ADT/placebo.
  • Patient relevance: Applies to a disease state where long-term treatment exposure and competing cardiovascular risk matter.
  • Main finding: Novel hormonal agents increased cardiovascular events and grade 3–4 hypertension risk compared with ADT plus placebo.
  • Limitations: Three RCTs; not all agents have identical risk profiles; trial selection may exclude high-risk patients.
  • Questions for clinician: Should cardiovascular risk alter monitoring intensity or drug selection?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• What is my baseline BP, and is it confirmed with home or ambulatory measurements?
  • What BP goal is appropriate for me, considering age, frailty, dizziness/falls, kidney disease, diabetes, and cardiovascular history?
  • Before ADT or androgen-receptor pathway therapy, have BP, lipids, glucose/HbA1c, kidney function, weight, and cardiovascular history been reviewed?
  • If abiraterone is used, how will BP, potassium, fluid retention/edema, and heart failure symptoms be monitored?
  • If enzalutamide/apalutamide/darolutamide is used, what is the plan for BP checks and cardiovascular symptom monitoring?
  • Should primary care, cardiology, or cardio-oncology be involved before starting systemic therapy?
  • Are lifestyle measures—salt intake, alcohol, exercise, weight, sleep apnea assessment—being handled alongside medication decisions?
  • Which symptoms require urgent contact: chest pain, shortness of breath, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, fainting, marked edema, or very high BP readings?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
This evidence adds a blood-pressure/cardiometabolic safety lens to the prostate cancer library:

  1. Incidence evidence — hypertension may be associated with fatal prostate cancer in some cohorts, but causality is uncertain.
  2. After diagnosis — prostate-specific recurrence evidence is limited; cardiovascular survivorship is the strongest reason to manage BP carefully.
  3. Treatment safety — ADT and androgen-receptor pathway inhibitors create the clearest clinical need for BP monitoring and management.
  4. Shared decision-making — treatment benefit should be weighed alongside cardiovascular history, BP control, frailty, and monitoring capacity.

docs.title: Exercise and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Category: Exercise Scope: exercise/physical activity before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes evidence for education and clinician discussion. It does not recommend any exercise program, cancer screening decision, or treatment choice. Exercise plans should be discussed with qualified clinicians, especially after surgery, during radiation or systemic therapy, with cardiovascular disease, severe fatigue, anemia, pain, falls risk, or possible bone metastases.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Phase Best-supported conclusion Strength of evidence Practical question for clinician Before diagnosis / incidence Physical activity is clearly beneficial for general health, but prostate-cancer-specific incidence findings are mixed. Meta-analyses suggest little or no association with total prostate cancer incidence; some studies suggest lower risk of advanced/aggressive or fatal disease. Moderate for "mixed/uncertain total incidence"; lower-to-moderate for advanced/fatal subtypes Does my overall health profile make exercise especially important regardless of uncertain prostate-cancer incidence effects? After diagnosis Observational cohorts consistently associate higher post-diagnosis activity, especially brisk walking/vigorous activity, with lower all-cause mortality and possibly lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality/progression. Moderate observational evidence; not proof of causation What activity level is safe for my risk group and treatment plan? During active treatment Randomized trials and reviews support aerobic and resistance exercise for fatigue, physical fitness, strength, body composition, and quality of life during radiation and ADT. These trials generally evaluate symptoms/function rather than cancer control. Moderate interventional evidence for function/QoL; limited for survival/cancer-control endpoints Should I be referred to physiotherapy or an exercise physiologist during treatment? After treatment / survivorship Exercise rehabilitation and pelvic-floor muscle training can help selected post-treatment problems, especially urinary incontinence after prostatectomy and fatigue/function after treatment. Moderate for pelvic-floor training; moderate/variable for broader rehabilitation outcomes What post-treatment exercise or pelvic-floor program is appropriate, supervised, and timed safely?

docs.title: Exercise types covered
docs.text:
• Walking / brisk walking: most clinically approachable activity in cohort evidence after diagnosis.
  • Vigorous activity: associated with better survival/progression outcomes in some cohorts, but subject to healthy-user and reverse-causation issues.
  • Aerobic training: RCT evidence during radiation/ADT supports fitness and fatigue/QoL outcomes.
  • Resistance training: RCT evidence supports muscle mass, strength, and function, especially relevant during ADT.
  • Combined aerobic + resistance programs: common in ADT/survivorship trials and exercise oncology guidance.
  • Pelvic-floor muscle exercise: specific post-prostatectomy urinary incontinence evidence; supervised programs appear more effective than unsupervised.

docs.title: 1. Before diagnosis: incidence and risk of developing prostate cancer
docs.text:
The most prostate-specific systematic reviews do not support a simple claim that exercise prevents all prostate cancer. Benke et al. (2018) found the highest vs lowest overall physical activity had a summary relative risk close to null for total prostate cancer incidence, while estimates for advanced disease trended lower but were not definitive. Liu et al. (2011) found a modest inverse association when combining observational studies, but results varied by design and measurement.

Large general-population cohort syntheses (Moore et al. 2016; Garcia et al. 2023) show strong benefits of physical activity for many chronic diseases and several cancers, but prostate cancer is not among the most consistently reduced cancer sites. Orsini et al. (2009) is a useful cohort suggesting lifetime activity, occupational activity, and walking/bicycling may be associated with lower prostate cancer incidence and mortality, but observational confounding remains important.

Interpretation: exercise should be viewed as strongly beneficial for overall health and treatment resilience, but not as a reliable prostate-cancer prevention strategy.

docs.title: 2. After diagnosis: progression, prostate-cancer mortality, and overall survival
docs.text:
The most directly relevant patient evidence is observational. Kenfield et al. (2011), Richman et al. (2011), Friedenreich et al. (2016), Wang et al. (2017), and the 2026 post-diagnosis meta-analysis all point in the same broad direction: men who are more physically active after diagnosis tend to have better overall survival, and in several analyses lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality or progression.

Key caution: these are not exercise-prescription trials powered for mortality. Men who feel well enough to exercise may differ in disease severity, comorbidity, treatment response, and health behaviors. Still, the consistency makes physical activity a reasonable topic for clinician discussion after diagnosis.

docs.title: 3. During active treatment: radiation and ADT
docs.text:
RCTs and systematic reviews support exercise as supportive care. Segal et al. (2009) tested resistance or aerobic exercise during radiation. Horgan and O'Donovan (2018) summarized RCTs during radiotherapy. For ADT, resistance and combined programs are especially relevant because ADT can worsen body composition, muscle, strength, metabolic risk, fatigue, and bone health. Houben et al. (2023), Alibhai et al. (2024), and Toohey et al. (2022) support feasibility and functional benefits of exercise programs in men on ADT.

Interpretation: exercise during treatment is best framed as symptom/function/risk-factor support, not as a proven anti-cancer treatment.

docs.title: 4. After treatment has ended: recovery and survivorship
docs.text:
Post-treatment evidence is strongest for specific functional outcomes. Pelvic-floor muscle exercise is relevant after radical prostatectomy; Baumann et al. (2022) found supervised pelvic-floor muscle exercise more effective than unsupervised exercise for urinary incontinence recovery. Broader exercise oncology guidance (Campbell et al. 2019) supports exercise to improve fatigue, physical function, and quality of life across cancer survivorship, with tailoring to cancer type, treatment effects, and safety risks.

Interpretation: after treatment, exercise is mainly a recovery, function, quality-of-life, and long-term health strategy. Evidence for recurrence/survival benefit is promising but mostly observational.

docs.title: Physical activity in relation to risk of prostate cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Benke IN, Leitzmann MF, Behrens G, Schmid D. Annals of Oncology. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1093/annonc/mdy073; PMID 29788165
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29788165/
  • Date: 2018 May 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.
  • Population: 48 cohort and 24 case-control studies; 151,748 prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Best prostate-specific synthesis for whether physical activity affects prostate cancer incidence.
  • Main finding: Overall physical activity was close to null for total prostate cancer incidence; estimates for advanced disease trended lower but were not definitive.
  • Limitations: Observational evidence, heterogeneous activity measures, detection/screening bias, and residual confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Should exercise be emphasized for general health and treatment resilience rather than as prostate cancer prevention?

docs.title: Does physical activity reduce the risk of prostate cancer? A systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Liu Y, Hu F, Li D, Wang F, Zhu L, Chen W. European Urology. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2011.07.007; PMID 21802197
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21802197/
  • Date: 2011 Nov
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of cohort and case-control studies.
  • Population: 19 cohort and 24 case-control studies; 88,294 prostate cancer cases.
  • Patient relevance: Earlier broad synthesis suggesting possible modest risk reduction.
  • Main finding: Combined observational data suggested total physical activity was associated with lower prostate cancer risk, but findings were inconsistent.
  • Limitations: Older evidence base, variable definitions of activity, observational design, possible publication and detection biases.
  • Questions for clinician: How should older positive evidence be balanced against newer meta-analyses showing weaker prostate-specific incidence associations?

docs.title: Non-occupational physical activity and risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer and mortality outcomes
docs.text:
• Citation: Garcia L, Pearce M, Abbas A, et al. British Journal of Sports Medicine. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1136/bjsports-2022-105669; PMID 36854652
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36854652/
  • Date: 2023 Aug
  • Category: Exercise; Nutrition/metabolic health context
  • Evidence type: Dose-response meta-analysis of large prospective studies.
  • Population: 196 articles; 94 cohorts; more than 30 million participants across multiple outcomes.
  • Patient relevance: Places prostate cancer in the broader context of exercise benefits for mortality, cardiovascular disease, and cancer.
  • Main finding: Non-occupational physical activity was associated with lower risks for many major outcomes; prostate-cancer-specific incidence benefit is less certain than for several other cancers.
  • Limitations: General-population evidence, not prostate-cancer-patient-specific; pooled studies vary in measurement and adjustment.
  • Questions for clinician: What level of activity is appropriate for cardiovascular and cancer survivorship health even if prostate cancer incidence effects are uncertain?

docs.title: Association of Leisure-Time Physical Activity With Risk of 26 Types of Cancer in 1.44 Million Adults
docs.text:
• Citation: Moore SC, Lee IM, Weiderpass E, et al. JAMA Internal Medicine. 2016.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamainternmed.2016.1548; PMID 27183032
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27183032/
  • Date: 2016 Jun 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Pooled prospective cohort analysis.
  • Population: 1.44 million adults from 12 US and European cohorts.
  • Patient relevance: Large-scale context for cancer prevention claims.
  • Main finding: Leisure-time physical activity was associated with lower risk of several cancers; prostate cancer was not one of the clearest protective associations.
  • Limitations: Self-reported activity, general population, screening/detection bias may differ by activity level.
  • Questions for clinician: Should exercise be discussed as broad cancer/cardiometabolic prevention rather than prostate-specific prevention?

docs.title: A prospective study of lifetime physical activity and prostate cancer incidence and mortality
docs.text:
• Citation: Orsini N, Bellocco R, Bottai M, et al. British Journal of Cancer. 2009.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/sj.bjc.6605404; PMID 19861965
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19861965/
  • Date: 2009 Dec 1
  • Category: Exercise; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: 45,887 Swedish men aged 45–79 years.
  • Patient relevance: Useful cohort on lifetime activity, walking/bicycling, occupational activity, incidence, and mortality.
  • Main finding: Higher lifetime total activity was associated with lower prostate cancer incidence; walking/bicycling and occupational activity showed inverse associations.
  • Limitations: Observational design, activity self-report, confounding, older cohort and screening context.
  • Questions for clinician: Does a history of activity change risk discussion, or mainly support general health advice?

docs.title: Physical activity and survival after prostate cancer diagnosis in the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
docs.text:
• Citation: Kenfield SA, Stampfer MJ, Giovannucci E, Chan JM. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.2010.31.5226; PMID 21205749
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21205749/
  • Date: 2011 Feb 20
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study after diagnosis.
  • Population: 2,705 men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer in the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study.
  • Patient relevance: Directly addresses activity after diagnosis and survival.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis physical activity was associated with lower all-cause mortality and lower prostate-cancer mortality; brisk walking and vigorous activity were notable.
  • Limitations: Observational; healthier men may be more active; not a randomized exercise prescription.
  • Questions for clinician: What level and type of walking or vigorous activity is safe after diagnosis and during the chosen management plan?

docs.title: Physical activity after diagnosis and risk of prostate cancer progression: CaPSURE
docs.text:
• Citation: Richman EL, Kenfield SA, Stampfer MJ, et al. Cancer Research. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1158/0008-5472.CAN-10-3932; PMID 21610110
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21610110/
  • Date: 2011 Jun 1
  • Category: Exercise; Active Surveillance; Risk Stratification & Staging
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort analysis.
  • Population: 1,455 men with clinically localized prostate cancer.
  • Patient relevance: Focuses on progression, an outcome relevant after diagnosis and during surveillance/treatment decision-making.
  • Main finding: Brisk walking after diagnosis was associated with lower risk of prostate cancer progression; walking pace appeared important.
  • Limitations: Observational, relatively few progression events, activity self-report, possible residual confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Is brisk walking appropriate during active surveillance or after local treatment, and are there safety limitations?

docs.title: Physical Activity and Survival After Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Friedenreich CM, Wang Q, Neilson HK, et al. European Urology. 2016.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2015.12.032; PMID 26774959
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26774959/
  • Date: 2016 Oct
  • Category: Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: 830 stage II–IV incident prostate cancer cases in Alberta, Canada, followed up to 17 years.
  • Patient relevance: Evaluates activity type, intensity, timing, survival, and recurrence/progression.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis activity was associated with better survival outcomes; activity timing and intensity mattered.
  • Limitations: Observational, self-reported activity, heterogeneous stages and treatments.
  • Questions for clinician: How should activity be adapted by stage, treatment, symptoms, and recurrence risk?

docs.title: Recreational Physical Activity in Relation to Prostate Cancer-specific Mortality Among Men with Nonmetastatic Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Wang Y, Jacobs EJ, Gapstur SM, et al. European Urology. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2017.06.037; PMID 28711382
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28711382/
  • Date: 2017 Dec
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study.
  • Population: Men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer in a US prospective cohort.
  • Patient relevance: Examines recreational activity, including walking, before and after diagnosis in relation to prostate-cancer-specific mortality.
  • Main finding: Higher recreational physical activity before and after diagnosis was associated with lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality in some analyses.
  • Limitations: Observational design, self-reported activity, potential reverse causation and confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Is a walking-based plan a safe baseline while more individualized exercise advice is arranged?

docs.title: Post-diagnosis physical activity in relation to mortality among prostate cancer survivors: a systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Benker P, Hamann A, Leitzmann MF, Stein MJ. Cancer Causes & Control. 2026.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s10552-026-02197-2; PMID 42348026
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42348026/
  • Date: 2026 Jun 25
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: 10 studies, more than 50,144 participants, 28,044 deaths.
  • Patient relevance: Current synthesis of post-diagnosis activity and mortality in prostate cancer survivors.
  • Main finding: Higher post-diagnosis physical activity was associated with lower all-cause mortality and lower prostate-cancer-specific mortality.
  • Limitations: Based on observational studies; activity measures and timing vary; causality not established.
  • Questions for clinician: What individualized, sustainable exercise target is reasonable after diagnosis?

docs.title: Randomized controlled trial of resistance or aerobic exercise in men receiving radiation therapy for prostate cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Segal RJ, Reid RD, Courneya KS, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2009.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.2007.15.4963; PMID 19064985
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19064985/
  • Date: 2009 Jan 20
  • Category: Exercise; Localized Treatment; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: 121 men receiving radiotherapy for prostate cancer, with or without ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Direct trial of aerobic vs resistance exercise during radiation.
  • Main finding: Exercise improved selected outcomes such as fatigue, fitness, strength, and quality of life measures compared with usual care; resistance and aerobic exercise had different strengths.
  • Limitations: Moderate sample size; endpoints are supportive-care outcomes, not survival or cancer control.
  • Questions for clinician: During radiation, should exercise emphasize aerobic training, resistance training, or both?

docs.title: The Impact of Exercise during Radiation Therapy for Prostate Cancer on Fatigue and Quality of Life
docs.text:
• Citation: Horgan S, O'Donovan A. Journal of Medical Imaging and Radiation Sciences. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.jmir.2018.02.056; PMID 32074040
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32074040/
  • Date: 2018 Jun
  • Category: Exercise; Localized Treatment; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer undergoing radiation therapy.
  • Patient relevance: Synthesizes exercise effects on radiation-related fatigue and quality of life.
  • Main finding: Exercise during radiation can help fatigue and quality of life, although effects vary by program and study.
  • Limitations: Limited number of trials, heterogeneous exercise prescriptions, supportive-care endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: What supervised or home exercise plan is safe during radiotherapy?

docs.title: Resistance Exercise Training Increases Muscle Mass and Strength in Prostate Cancer Patients on ADT
docs.text:
• Citation: Houben LHP, Overkamp M, van Kraaij P, et al. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1249/MSS.0000000000003095; PMID 36534950
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36534950/
  • Date: 2023 Apr 1
  • Category: Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Randomized exercise trial.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer receiving ADT.
  • Patient relevance: ADT commonly worsens muscle, strength, body composition, and metabolic health.
  • Main finding: Resistance exercise training increased muscle mass and strength in men on ADT; protein supplementation did not appear to be the main driver compared with exercise.
  • Limitations: Functional/supportive endpoints rather than cancer-control endpoints; program supervision and adherence affect applicability.
  • Questions for clinician: Should resistance training be part of an ADT supportive-care plan, and is referral to an exercise professional appropriate?

docs.title: Home-based versus supervised group exercise in men with prostate cancer on ADT
docs.text:
• Citation: Alibhai SMH, Papadopoulos E, Mina DS, et al. Journal of Geriatric Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.jgo.2023.101646; PMID 37976654
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37976654/
  • Date: 2024 Jan
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease
  • Evidence type: Multicentre randomized controlled trial and economic analysis.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer on ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Compares practical delivery models: home-based vs supervised group exercise.
  • Main finding: Both home and group models were used for aerobic and resistance training; the study informs feasibility, adherence, outcomes, and costs.
  • Limitations: Delivery model and local resources may affect generalizability; not powered for cancer survival endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: Is supervised group exercise necessary, or could a structured home program be sufficient and safe?

docs.title: Exercise Adherence in Men with Prostate Cancer Undergoing ADT: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Toohey K, Hunter M, Paterson C, Mortazavi R, Singh B. Cancers. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3390/cancers14102452; PMID 35626058
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35626058/
  • Date: 2022 May 16
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Advanced & Recurrent Disease
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: Men with prostate cancer undergoing ADT in exercise intervention studies.
  • Patient relevance: Helps assess whether ADT exercise programs are realistic and beneficial.
  • Main finding: Exercise interventions in men on ADT are feasible and can improve physiological outcomes; adherence and patient experience are central.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneity in programs, supervision, adherence measurement, and outcomes.
  • Questions for clinician: What program design would maximize adherence for my circumstances?

docs.title: Clinical exercise interventions in prostate cancer patients — systematic review of randomized controlled trials
docs.text:
• Citation: Baumann FT, Zopf EM, Bloch W. Supportive Care in Cancer. 2012.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s00520-011-1271-0; PMID 21989678
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21989678/
  • Date: 2012 Feb
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of randomized controlled trials.
  • Population: Prostate cancer patients during treatment and aftercare.
  • Patient relevance: Broad review of exercise interventions addressing common problems such as fatigue, incontinence, function, and quality of life.
  • Main finding: RCTs generally show positive physiological and psychological effects of exercise interventions in prostate cancer care.
  • Limitations: Older review; trials vary widely in intervention type, treatment phase, and outcome measures.
  • Questions for clinician: Which exercise goals matter most now: fatigue, continence, strength, aerobic fitness, mood, or return to activities?

docs.title: Supervised pelvic floor muscle exercise after radical prostatectomy — systematic review and meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Baumann FT, Reimer N, Gockeln T, et al. Disability and Rehabilitation. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1080/09638288.2021.1937717; PMID 34550846
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34550846/
  • Date: 2022 Sep
  • Category: Exercise; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Localized Treatment
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis.
  • Population: Men after radical prostatectomy.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant to post-surgical urinary incontinence recovery.
  • Main finding: Supervised pelvic-floor muscle exercise was more effective than unsupervised exercise for improving urinary incontinence after radical prostatectomy.
  • Limitations: Program details and timing vary; outcomes depend on adherence and baseline severity.
  • Questions for clinician: Should pelvic-floor physiotherapy start before or after surgery, and should it be supervised?

Practical patient resource: Five exercises to strengthen your pelvic floor, UT Health San Antonio MD Anderson Cancer Center. This is a practical exercise-description page rather than a research paper; use it as a discussion aid with a treating clinician or pelvic-floor physiotherapist, especially after prostate surgery.

docs.title: Exercise Guidelines for Cancer Survivors: Consensus Statement
docs.text:
• Citation: Campbell KL, Winters-Stone KM, Wiskemann J, et al. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1249/MSS.0000000000002116; PMID 31626055
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31626055/
  • Date: 2019 Nov
  • Category: Exercise; Guidelines & Care Pathways; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: International multidisciplinary consensus statement.
  • Population: Cancer survivors broadly, with relevance to prostate cancer survivorship and treatment side effects.
  • Patient relevance: Provides practical exercise-oncology framing and safety principles after diagnosis and treatment.
  • Main finding: Exercise is generally safe for cancer survivors when appropriately tailored and can improve fatigue, quality of life, physical function, anxiety, and depressive symptoms.
  • Limitations: Not prostate-only; individual contraindications and treatment-specific risks require clinician input.
  • Questions for clinician: Should I be referred to an accredited exercise physiologist or physiotherapist with oncology experience?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• Which phase am I in: screening, active surveillance, surgery recovery, radiation, ADT/systemic therapy, or survivorship?
  • Are there any safety limits: bone metastases, anemia, infection, cardiac risk, falls risk, pain, severe fatigue, urinary catheter/wound recovery, or treatment complications?
  • Should the plan emphasize walking, aerobic training, resistance training, pelvic-floor therapy, flexibility/balance, or a combination?
  • Would supervised exercise physiology/physiotherapy be safer or more effective than a home program?
  • What outcomes should be tracked: fatigue, strength, continence, sexual function, bowel symptoms, weight/body composition, glucose/lipids, mood, or PSA/treatment milestones?
  • What symptoms should prompt stopping exercise and contacting the clinical team?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
This evidence strengthens the Exercise category by adding a structured distinction between:

  1. Incidence prevention claims — mixed and should be stated cautiously.
  2. Post-diagnosis prognosis associations — promising but mainly observational.
  3. Supportive-care exercise during treatment — stronger randomized evidence for fatigue, fitness, strength, function, and quality of life.
  4. Post-treatment rehabilitation — strongest for targeted problems such as urinary incontinence after prostatectomy and general survivorship function.

docs.title: Immune Modulation and Anti-inflammatory Agents in Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04

Category: Therapies; advanced & recurrent disease; localized treatment; side effects & quality of life Scope: Patient-facing review of immune modulation and anti-inflammatory approaches in prostate-cancer management, from approved immunotherapy to investigational vaccines, checkpoint inhibitors, mycobacterial immune modulators, NSAIDs/COX-2 inhibitors, vitamin D, low-dose naltrexone, bromelain, corticosteroids, and inflammation-related trial strategies.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes evidence for discussion with clinicians. It does not recommend starting aspirin, NSAIDs, celecoxib, low-dose naltrexone, bromelain, vitamin D, immunotherapy, supplements, or off-label immune-modulating regimens for prostate cancer. These agents can have serious risks, drug interactions, bleeding/cardiovascular effects, immune toxicities, or may delay proven cancer therapy if used outside a supervised plan.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Question State of the evidence Clinician discussion question Is prostate cancer an immunotherapy-responsive cancer? Usually less responsive than melanoma, lung cancer, kidney cancer, and some mismatch-repair-deficient tumors. Most unselected prostate cancers are relatively "immune cold." "Has my tumor been tested for MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-high, CDK12, HRR alterations, PD-L1, or other markers that might affect immunotherapy relevance?" Is any immunotherapy approved with survival evidence? Sipuleucel-T improved overall survival by about 4 months in asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), without clearly delaying radiographic/clinical progression. Availability varies by country. "If I have mCRPC and few symptoms, is sipuleucel-T available and appropriate in my care system?" Do checkpoint inhibitors work for most prostate cancer? Not for most unselected patients. Pembrolizumab has low response rates in unselected mCRPC, but can produce meaningful responses in tumors with MSI-H/dMMR or selected high-TMB biology. "Do my genomic results suggest a tumor-agnostic indication for pembrolizumab or a clinical trial?" Do combination checkpoint strategies solve the problem? Mixed and mostly investigational. Several phase 3 combinations have not improved overall survival in biomarker-unselected mCRPC, although some combinations show progression-free-survival or subgroup signals. "Is this combination guideline-supported for my biomarker group, or is it mainly a clinical-trial question?" Do anti-inflammatory drugs such as aspirin/NSAIDs improve prostate-cancer outcomes? Observational studies are inconsistent; randomized evidence does not support routine use as prostate-cancer treatment. Aspirin/NSAIDs may be indicated for cardiovascular or pain reasons, but cancer-control benefit is unproven. "Do I have a cardiovascular, bleeding, kidney, ulcer, anticoagulant, or perioperative reason to use or avoid aspirin/NSAIDs?" What about celecoxib/COX-2 inhibition? STAMPEDE and perioperative biomarker studies do not show clear survival or biomarker benefit sufficient for routine prostate-cancer treatment. Celecoxib has cardiovascular and renal risks. "Is there any cancer-specific reason for celecoxib in my case, or would it only be used for pain/inflammation under usual safety rules?" What about vitamin D, bromelain, or low-dose naltrexone? Vitamin D deficiency should be corrected for general health, but high-dose vitamin D has not shown reliable prostate-cancer treatment benefit. Bromelain and low-dose naltrexone are hypothesis-generating, not standard-of-care cancer therapy. "Should my vitamin D be checked/corrected, and could any supplement interact with cancer treatment, anticoagulants, surgery, or pain medications?" How should the Dalgleish/Liu IMM-101 case report be interpreted? It is interesting and hypothesis-generating, but it is a six-patient uncontrolled case report with major confounding from standard therapies. It cannot prove that IMM-101, LDN, bromelain, aspirin, or vitamin D controlled cancer. "Is there a registered clinical trial or controlled evidence for this regimen, and would it interfere with proven therapy?"

docs.title: 1. Why immune modulation is biologically plausible but clinically difficult
docs.text:
Prostate cancer is shaped by androgen signaling, DNA-repair biology, bone microenvironment, myeloid immune suppression, chronic inflammation, tumor-associated macrophages, regulatory T-cell activity, and therapy-induced immune changes. This makes immune modulation scientifically plausible. However, most prostate cancers have relatively low tumor mutational burden, limited T-cell infiltration, low PD-L1 expression, and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment compared with cancers where checkpoint inhibitors are broadly effective.

Practical interpretation: immune modulation is not one idea. It includes:

  • therapeutic vaccines and cellular immunotherapy;
  • checkpoint inhibitors such as PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4 blockade;
  • combinations with androgen-receptor pathway inhibitors, PARP inhibitors, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, or tyrosine-kinase inhibitors;
  • macrophage/myeloid/TGF-β/IL-8 and other microenvironment-directed strategies;
  • anti-inflammatory and repurposed agents such as aspirin, NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors, corticosteroids, vitamin D, low-dose naltrexone, and supplements.

These have very different evidence levels.

docs.title: Sipuleucel-T: proven survival signal in a selected mCRPC setting
docs.text:
Sipuleucel-T is an autologous cellular immunotherapy studied in asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC. In the pivotal IMPACT trial, sipuleucel-T reduced risk of death compared with placebo and improved median overall survival:

  • median overall survival: 25.8 vs 21.7 months;
  • hazard ratio for death: 0.78;
  • 36-month survival: 31.7% vs 23.0%;
  • no clear effect on time to objective disease progression.[^sipuleucel]

Patient interpretation: sipuleucel-T is the clearest example that immune modulation can help selected prostate-cancer patients, but it is not a rapid tumor-shrinking therapy and is most relevant before heavy symptoms or very rapidly progressive disease. Access differs substantially by country.

docs.title: Pembrolizumab: biomarker-selected role, not broad unselected use
docs.text:
In the KEYNOTE-199 phase 2 study of pembrolizumab in treatment-refractory mCRPC, objective response rates were low in unselected cohorts:

  • 5% in PD-L1-positive measurable disease;
  • 3% in PD-L1-negative measurable disease;
  • some responses were durable.[^keynote199]

The strongest clinical rationale for pembrolizumab is not "prostate cancer" by itself, but tumor biology such as MSI-H/dMMR and, in some settings, high tumor mutational burden. A 2024 analysis reported that among patients with MSI-H/dMMR prostate cancer treated with immune checkpoint blockade, about 45% had RECIST responses and 65% had PSA50 responses, while TMB-high/microsatellite-stable disease looked less reliably responsive.[^msi-tmb]

Patient interpretation: if considering immunotherapy, the important step is tumor genomic profiling and pathology review, not assuming checkpoint inhibitors work for prostate cancer generally.

docs.title: 3. Checkpoint-inhibitor combinations: active research, mixed results
CTLA-4 blockade with ipilimumab
docs.text:
A phase 3 trial of ipilimumab after radiotherapy in post-docetaxel mCRPC did not meet its primary overall-survival endpoint:

  • median overall survival 11.2 vs 10.0 months;
  • HR 0.85, p=0.053;
  • grade 3–4 immune-related adverse events occurred in 26% with ipilimumab vs 3% with placebo.[^ipilimumab]

Nivolumab plus ipilimumab has shown activity in CheckMate 650, with objective response rates reported around 25% pre-chemotherapy and 10% post-chemotherapy in an early analysis, but grade 3–4 treatment-related adverse events were common and treatment-related deaths occurred.[^checkmate650]

Patient interpretation: dual checkpoint blockade is biologically interesting but toxicity is real, and use should generally be trial- or biomarker-driven.

docs.title: PD-L1 blockade plus androgen-receptor pathway inhibition
docs.text:
The IMbassador250 phase 3 trial tested atezolizumab plus enzalutamide versus enzalutamide alone after abiraterone in mCRPC. It did not improve overall survival in an unselected population:

  • OS HR 1.12;
  • p=0.28.[^imbassador250]

Biomarker analyses suggested immune-active subgroups may exist, but this is not the same as a standard indication for all patients.

docs.title: PD-1 plus PARP inhibition
docs.text:
KEYLYNK-010 tested pembrolizumab plus olaparib in biomarker-unselected, previously treated mCRPC. It did not significantly improve radiographic progression-free survival or overall survival compared with a next-generation hormonal agent:

  • rPFS 4.4 vs 4.2 months;
  • OS 15.8 vs 14.6 months;
  • grade ≥3 treatment-related adverse events 34.6% vs 9.0%.[^keylynk010]

Patient interpretation: combinations that sound mechanistically plausible still need randomized evidence and biomarker selection.

docs.title: Cabozantinib plus atezolizumab
docs.text:
CONTACT-02 tested cabozantinib plus atezolizumab versus ARPI switch in mCRPC after prior ARPI. Final analysis showed improved progression-free survival but not overall survival:

  • PFS 6.3 vs 4.2 months, HR 0.65;
  • OS 14.8 vs 15.0 months, HR 0.89, p=0.30;
  • grade 3–4 adverse events 56% vs 26%.[^contact02]

Patient interpretation: a PFS signal may be clinically relevant in some contexts, but lack of OS benefit and higher toxicity require careful discussion.

docs.title: 4. Therapeutic vaccines and immune adjuvants beyond sipuleucel-T
docs.text:
PROSTVAC, a viral-vector immunotherapy, had promising phase 2 survival results but failed in a phase 3 trial of asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC:

  • median OS 34.4 months with PROSTVAC, 33.2 months with PROSTVAC plus GM-CSF, and 34.3 months with placebo;
  • no OS benefit.[^prostvac]

This pattern—promising immune signal, then neutral phase 3 result—is common in prostate-cancer immunotherapy.

Patient interpretation: vaccines may still matter in future combinations or selected disease states, but most non-approved vaccine/adjuvant regimens remain investigational.

docs.title: 5. The supplied case report: IMM-101 plus anti-inflammatory/immune-modulatory agents
docs.text:
The paper Terry highlighted is:

Dalgleish AG, Liu WM. The role of immune modulation and anti-inflammatory agents in the management of prostate cancer: A case report of six patients. Oncology Letters. 2022. DOI 10.3892/ol.2022.13367; PMID 35761946.[^dalgleish-liu]

docs.title: What the authors reported
docs.text:
The paper describes six prostate-cancer patients treated in a named-patient context with IMM-101, a heat-killed whole-cell mycobacterial preparation, alongside conventional treatments and various partner agents:

  • vitamin D3 correction;
  • aspirin;
  • bromelain;
  • low-dose naltrexone (LDN);
  • zoledronic acid;
  • standard prostate-cancer therapies such as hormone therapy, radiotherapy, docetaxel, abiraterone, enzalutamide, radium-223, and cabazitaxel in some cases.

The authors report PSA declines or slowed PSA doubling time, stable imaging for periods of time, and few side effects attributed to IMM-101/LDN. Their table summarizes six cases and partner agents: zoledronic acid, aspirin, bromelain, LDN, or combinations.

docs.title: Why it is interesting
docs.text:
• It connects a plausible biologic idea—mycobacterial immune activation plus anti-inflammatory modulation—with prostate-cancer disease control.
  • It highlights a research question: whether innate immune stimulation, myeloid modulation, or inflammatory-pathway modulation could make prostate cancer more immune-responsive.
  • It is relevant to patients who are asking about non-standard adjunctive therapies.

docs.title: Why it should not be treated as proof of efficacy
docs.text:
Limitation Why it matters Only six patients Case reports can generate hypotheses but cannot estimate treatment effect. No control group The natural course of PSA kinetics and disease biology cannot be separated from treatment effect. Major confounding by standard therapies Patients also received ADT, radiotherapy, docetaxel, abiraterone, enzalutamide, radium-223, cabazitaxel, and zoledronic acid at different times. Multiple simultaneous adjuncts IMM-101, vitamin D, aspirin, bromelain, LDN, and other agents were not isolated from each other. Selection and reporting bias Named-patient experiences may overrepresent unusual responders or highly motivated patients. Surrogate outcomes PSA changes and scan stability are useful signals, but they do not prove improved survival or quality of life. Competing interests The paper discloses patents related to low-dose naltrexone as a cancer therapy.

Repository interpretation: this case report belongs in the dashboard as a hypothesis-generating signal, not as a treatment recommendation. It supports asking whether controlled trials exist; it does not justify substituting IMM-101/LDN/bromelain for proven prostate-cancer therapy.

docs.title: Aspirin and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
docs.text:
Epidemiologic studies have explored whether aspirin or NSAIDs reduce prostate-cancer incidence, recurrence, or mortality. Results are inconsistent. Meta-analyses of aspirin exposure after diagnosis have not shown a reliable prostate-cancer-specific mortality benefit.[^aspirin-mortality] Earlier NSAID-risk meta-analyses described evidence as suggestive but not conclusive.[^nsaid-risk]

Safety issue: aspirin and NSAIDs can cause gastrointestinal bleeding, kidney injury, perioperative bleeding, interactions with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, and cardiovascular risks depending on agent and patient context.

Patient interpretation: aspirin should be discussed primarily as a cardiovascular/vascular-risk medication unless a clinician is using it for another clear indication. It should not be started solely for prostate-cancer control based on current evidence.

docs.title: Celecoxib and COX-2 inhibition
docs.text:
COX-2 is biologically relevant to inflammation, angiogenesis, and tumor signaling, but randomized clinical evidence has not established celecoxib as routine prostate-cancer therapy.

In STAMPEDE, adding celecoxib to standard hormone therapy did not improve overall survival in the overall trial population. Long-term survival results reported:

  • SOC + celecoxib HR for death 0.98, p=0.847;
  • SOC + zoledronic acid + celecoxib HR 0.86, p=0.130;
  • conclusion: no overall evidence of improved survival with celecoxib.[^stampedecel]

Perioperative randomized celecoxib biomarker trials in localized prostate cancer also failed to show convincing biomarker benefit, despite measurable prostate tissue levels.[^celecoxib-preop]

Patient interpretation: celecoxib is not a standard anti-cancer therapy for prostate cancer. If used for pain/inflammation, cardiovascular, renal, gastrointestinal, and perioperative risks matter.

docs.title: Corticosteroids: supportive and treatment-partner role, not simple immune enhancement
docs.text:
Prednisone/prednisolone is routinely paired with abiraterone to reduce mineralocorticoid excess and is also used with docetaxel/cabazitaxel regimens and for symptom control in some advanced settings. In LATITUDE, abiraterone acetate plus prednisone with ADT improved overall survival in newly diagnosed high-risk metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer.[^latitude]

But prednisone is not being used there as an anti-inflammatory cancer cure. It is part of a regimen that modifies androgen synthesis and manages toxicity.

Patient interpretation: corticosteroids can be necessary and beneficial in standard regimens, but long-term risks include hyperglycemia, infection risk, bone loss, muscle weakness, mood/sleep effects, and adrenal suppression.

docs.title: Vitamin D
docs.text:
Correcting deficiency is reasonable for bone health and general health, particularly in men receiving ADT who face bone-density and metabolic risks. However, vitamin D supplementation has not shown reliable prostate-cancer treatment benefit in controlled trials. A systematic review/meta-analysis of clinical trials found no significant improvement in PSA change, PSA response proportion, or mortality in controlled trials and concluded high-dose vitamin D should not be recommended as prostate-cancer treatment.[^vitd]

Patient interpretation: ask whether vitamin D should be measured and corrected to a safe target, but do not treat vitamin D as a proven anti-cancer therapy.

docs.title: Low-dose naltrexone and bromelain
docs.text:
LDN and bromelain appear in the Dalgleish/Liu case report as anti-inflammatory or immune-modulatory adjuncts. LDN has preclinical and mechanistic literature, and the authors discuss TLR-9 and cytokine-modulating hypotheses. Bromelain is also discussed as an anti-inflammatory agent. However, controlled prostate-cancer outcome evidence is insufficient.

Safety issue: LDN can interfere with opioid pain medicines and perioperative analgesia. Bromelain may have bleeding or drug-interaction concerns, especially around anticoagulants/antiplatelets or surgery.

Patient interpretation: these are not standard prostate-cancer treatments. They should only be discussed as experimental adjuncts or trial questions, with medication-interaction review.

docs.title: 7. How to use this evidence in clinician discussion
Questions for the oncology team
docs.text:
• Biomarkers: Has my tumor been tested for MSI-H/dMMR, TMB, CDK12, HRR genes such as BRCA1/2, PTEN/PI3K pathway changes, PD-L1, or other immune-relevant markers?
  • Disease state: Am I hormone-sensitive, non-metastatic castration-resistant, metastatic castration-resistant, post-docetaxel, post-ARPI, or biomarker-selected?
  • Approved therapy: Is sipuleucel-T available and appropriate for my disease state and symptom burden?
  • Checkpoint inhibitors: Is pembrolizumab relevant because of a tumor-agnostic biomarker indication, or would it be off-label/clinical-trial only?
  • Trials: Are there trials combining immunotherapy with radiotherapy, PARP inhibition, PSMA radioligand therapy, vaccines, myeloid modulation, or androgen-receptor pathway therapy?
  • Anti-inflammatory drugs: Do I have a separate cardiovascular, pain, arthritis, or post-radiation urinary indication for aspirin/NSAIDs/celecoxib, and what are the bleeding/kidney/cardiac risks?
  • Supplements and LDN: Could vitamin D, bromelain, LDN, or other supplements interfere with surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, anticoagulants, opioids, or clinical-trial eligibility?
  • Evidence threshold: Is the proposed immune/anti-inflammatory strategy supported by randomized prostate-cancer data, a biomarker indication, or only case reports/preclinical rationale?

docs.title: Red flags for patient safety
docs.text:
• Starting aspirin/NSAIDs before biopsy, surgery, or while on anticoagulants without clinician approval.
  • Using high-dose supplements or LDN without disclosing them to the oncology team.
  • Treating PSA improvement from a multi-agent regimen as proof that an unproven adjunct worked.
  • Delaying ADT, radiotherapy, surgery, chemotherapy, radioligand therapy, PARP inhibitor therapy, or other proven treatments to try unproven immune-modulatory regimens.
  • Assuming "anti-inflammatory" means harmless.

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
The state of the art is best summarized as:

  1. Proven but narrow: sipuleucel-T has randomized overall-survival evidence in selected mCRPC.
  2. Biomarker-selected: pembrolizumab and checkpoint inhibitors are most credible for MSI-H/dMMR and select high-TMB biology, not for most unselected prostate cancers.
  3. Combination research: checkpoint combinations are active but have produced mixed results; many phase 3 unselected strategies have not improved overall survival.
  4. Anti-inflammatory adjuncts: aspirin/NSAIDs/celecoxib/vitamin D/LDN/bromelain are not established prostate-cancer control therapies. Some may be appropriate for non-cancer indications or deficiency correction.
  5. Case-report signal: IMM-101 plus anti-inflammatory/immune-modulatory agents is hypothesis-generating and worth tracking for trials, but not practice-changing without controlled evidence.

docs.title: Kantoff et al. 2010 — sipuleucel-T IMPACT trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Kantoff PW, et al. New England Journal of Medicine. 2010.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1001294; PMID 20818862
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized trial.
  • Population: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC.
  • Main finding: Overall survival improved by 4.1 months; no clear effect on objective disease progression.
  • Patient relevance: Most established immunotherapy survival signal in prostate cancer.

docs.title: Antonarakis et al. 2020 — KEYNOTE-199 pembrolizumab
docs.text:
• Citation: Antonarakis ES, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2020.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.19.01638; PMID 31774688
  • Evidence type: Phase 2 multicohort study.
  • Population: Treatment-refractory mCRPC.
  • Main finding: Low objective response rates in unselected/PD-L1-defined cohorts; some durable responses.
  • Patient relevance: Supports biomarker testing rather than broad checkpoint-inhibitor use.

docs.title: Kwon et al. 2014 — ipilimumab after radiotherapy
docs.text:
• Citation: Kwon ED, et al. Lancet Oncology. 2014.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(14)70189-5; PMID 24831977
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized trial.
  • Population: mCRPC after docetaxel.
  • Main finding: Primary OS endpoint not met; immune-related grade 3–4 toxicity increased.
  • Patient relevance: Checkpoint activation can be toxic and is not automatically beneficial.

docs.title: Fizazi et al. 2022 — IMbassador250 atezolizumab plus enzalutamide
docs.text:
• Citation: Fizazi K, et al. Nature Medicine. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/s41591-021-01600-6; PMID 35013615
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized trial.
  • Population: mCRPC after abiraterone.
  • Main finding: No OS improvement in the unselected population; biomarker subgroup signals require further study.
  • Patient relevance: Combination immunotherapy requires evidence and selection.

docs.title: Yu et al. 2023 — KEYLYNK-010 pembrolizumab plus olaparib
docs.text:
• Citation: Yu EY, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.23.00233; PMID 37290035
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized trial.
  • Population: Biomarker-unselected, previously treated mCRPC.
  • Main finding: No significant rPFS or OS improvement; higher grade ≥3 treatment-related adverse events.
  • Patient relevance: PARP/immunotherapy combinations are not automatically useful without biomarker context.

docs.title: Gulley et al. 2019 — PROSTVAC phase 3
docs.text:
• Citation: Gulley JL, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.18.02031; PMID 30817251
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized vaccine trial.
  • Population: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC.
  • Main finding: No OS benefit compared with placebo.
  • Patient relevance: Promising vaccine concepts still need phase 3 proof.

docs.title: Dalgleish and Liu 2022 — IMM-101 case report
docs.text:
• Citation: Dalgleish AG, Liu WM. Oncology Letters. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3892/ol.2022.13367; PMID 35761946
  • Evidence type: Six-patient case report.
  • Population: Prostate-cancer patients receiving IMM-101 plus conventional and adjunctive therapies.
  • Main finding: Reported PSA/imaging stability signals and few side effects.
  • Limitations: No control group; heavy confounding; multiple simultaneous interventions; patent conflicts disclosed.
  • Patient relevance: Useful for questions and trial tracking, not for proving efficacy.

docs.title: James et al. 2017 — STAMPEDE celecoxib survival results
docs.text:
• Citation: James ND, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.2016.69.0677; PMID 28300506
  • Evidence type: Adaptive multiarm randomized platform trial.
  • Population: Hormone-naive advanced/high-risk prostate cancer starting long-term hormone therapy.
  • Main finding: No overall evidence of improved survival with celecoxib.
  • Patient relevance: Strong caution against using celecoxib as routine prostate-cancer therapy.

docs.title: Harrison et al. 2017 — pre-prostatectomy celecoxib biomarker trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Harrison DJ, et al. BJU International. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/bju.13612; PMID 27480340
  • Evidence type: Double-blind randomized pre-prostatectomy study.
  • Population: Localized prostate cancer.
  • Main finding: No effect on apoptosis, prostaglandins, androgen receptor levels, or PSA; one postoperative MI in celecoxib arm.
  • Patient relevance: Biomarker and safety caution for COX-2 inhibition.

docs.title: Liu et al. 2019 — aspirin mortality meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Liu Y, et al. BioMed Research International. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: PMID 31073532
  • Evidence type: Systematic review/meta-analysis.
  • Population: Prostate-cancer patients exposed to aspirin before or after diagnosis.
  • Main finding: No significant association between aspirin exposure and prostate-cancer-specific or all-cause mortality in highest-exposure analyses.
  • Patient relevance: Aspirin should not be assumed to improve cancer outcomes.

docs.title: Shahvazi et al. 2019 — vitamin D supplementation meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Shahvazi S, et al. Hormone and Metabolic Research. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1055/a-0774-8809; PMID 30522147
  • Evidence type: Systematic review/meta-analysis of clinical trials.
  • Population: Prostate-cancer patients receiving vitamin D supplementation.
  • Main finding: Controlled trials did not show significant benefit in PSA change, PSA response, or mortality.
  • Patient relevance: Correct deficiency for general health; do not treat vitamin D as proven cancer therapy.

docs.title: References

docs.title: STIs, UTIs, Prostatitis, and PSA Levels: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Categories: Diagnostic Workup; Screening & Early Detection; Risk Stratification & Staging Scope: sexually transmitted infections (STIs), urinary tract infections (UTIs), prostatitis/prostate inflammation, systemic infections, and interpretation of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) results.

Not medical advice

This page is for education and clinician discussion. It does not diagnose infection or prostate cancer, and it does not advise delaying investigation of an abnormal PSA. A PSA result should be interpreted with a qualified clinician, especially if there are urinary symptoms, fever, pelvic pain, recent infection, recent urinary instrumentation, abnormal digital rectal examination, rapidly rising PSA, or other cancer risk factors.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Question Best-supported conclusion Practical implication Can infections raise PSA? Yes. Acute prostatitis and febrile UTI can markedly raise PSA; STI-related and systemic infections may also be associated with PSA rises in some studies. Tell the clinician about recent UTI, prostatitis symptoms, STI exposure/diagnosis, fever/systemic infection, antibiotics, urinary retention, catheterization, cystoscopy, or biopsy before interpreting PSA. Can PSA stay elevated after infection? Yes. PSA often falls after treatment/recovery, but some studies show a slow decline over weeks to months; one longitudinal STI/systemic infection paper found higher likelihood of PSA increase over ~10 years after infection history. A repeat PSA after clinical recovery may be appropriate, but timing should be individualized. Persistent or high-risk PSA patterns still need proper evaluation. Do STIs/UTIs explain away prostate cancer risk? No. Infection/inflammation can be a confounder, not a guarantee that PSA is benign. Infection and cancer can coexist. Avoid assuming “it is only infection”; ask what follow-up PSA, urinalysis/culture, MRI, urology review, or biopsy threshold applies. Should asymptomatic elevated PSA be treated with antibiotics just to lower PSA? Evidence does not support routine empiric antibiotics for asymptomatic elevated PSA without clinical suspicion of infection. Antibiotics should be used for likely/confirmed infection, not as a general PSA-lowering test.

docs.title: Why this matters for diagnosis
docs.text:
PSA is prostate-specific, not cancer-specific. It can rise when prostate tissue is inflamed, infected, mechanically irritated, enlarged, or malignant. STIs, UTIs, and prostatitis matter because they can create false-positive concern or complicate timing of PSA interpretation.

For a patient considering prostate cancer diagnosis, the key issue is not whether infection can raise PSA—it can—but whether the PSA pattern after recovery still warrants cancer evaluation.

docs.title: 1. Febrile UTI and acute prostatitis can substantially elevate PSA
docs.text:
The strongest and most clinically direct evidence is for febrile UTI and prostatitis. In a prospective BJU International study of men with febrile UTI, 83% had elevated PSA during the acute illness, with a median PSA of 14 ng/mL and a range up to 140 ng/mL. PSA declined rapidly in many men after one month, but some had a prolonged decrease, suggesting a slow healing process.

Interpretation: if PSA is measured during febrile UTI, acute prostatitis, urinary retention, or acute urinary symptoms, it may substantially overstate cancer suspicion. However, the appropriate response is clinician-guided follow-up, not ignoring the result.

docs.title: 2. STIs may affect PSA through prostate inflammation or cell damage
docs.text:
The included PMC paper, Sustained influence of infections on prostate-specific antigen concentration, examined young active-duty US servicemen with chlamydia, gonorrhea, non-chlamydial/non-gonococcal urethritis, infectious mononucleosis, and other infections. Over an average of 10 years, men with STI histories, systemic infections, or any infections were more likely to have PSA increases than controls.

The authors interpret PSA as a marker of prostate inflammation and cell damage and suggest both genitourinary and systemic infections may influence the prostate. This does not prove infection causes prostate cancer, but it supports considering infection history when interpreting PSA.

docs.title: 3. Population evidence for sexual behavior/STIs and PSA is mixed
docs.text: A nationally representative US cross-sectional study of men aged 40–59 did not find consistent evidence that sexual behavior or self-reported/laboratory STI measures were associated with PSA or percent-free PSA. This suggests STI-related PSA effects may be more evident in selected populations, recent/protracted infections, or subgroups with higher infection burden rather than in the general middle-aged population.

docs.title: 4. Antibiotics are not a routine solution for asymptomatic PSA elevation
docs.text: Although prostatitis/inflammation can elevate PSA, a randomized trial of empiric ciprofloxacin for asymptomatic men with elevated PSA and normal digital rectal examination did not show a meaningful PSA-lowering benefit compared with no antibiotic. This supports antimicrobial stewardship: treat suspected or confirmed infection, but do not use antibiotics simply to “test” whether PSA will fall.

docs.title: Sustained influence of infections on prostate-specific antigen concentration: an analysis of changes over 10 years of follow-up
docs.text:
• Citation: Langston ME, et al. The Prostate. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID/PMCID: DOI 10.1002/pros.23660; PMID 30133756; PMCID PMC6690490
  • URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6690490/
  • Date: 2018 May 30 online / 2018 Sep issue
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Longitudinal analysis using stored serum specimens and medical record infection diagnoses.
  • Population: Young active-duty US servicemen: chlamydia, gonorrhea, non-chlamydial/non-gonococcal urethritis, infectious mononucleosis, other systemic/non-genitourinary infections, and controls.
  • Patient relevance: Directly addresses whether infection history can affect PSA beyond the acute illness period.
  • Main finding: Over ~10 years, PSA increases were more common in men with histories of STIs (67.7% vs 60.8%), systemic infections (66.7% vs 54.4%), or any infection (68.5% vs 54.4%) compared with controls.
  • Limitations: Young military population; PSA changes were generally not equivalent to a prostate-cancer screening population; observational design; infection burden may correlate with other factors.
  • Questions for clinician: If I recently had an STI, UTI, prostatitis symptoms, or systemic infection, should PSA be repeated after recovery before deciding next steps?

docs.title: Prostatic involvement in men with febrile urinary tract infection as measured by serum PSA and transrectal ultrasonography
docs.text:
• Citation: Ulleryd P, et al. BJU International. 1999.
  • PMID: 10468764
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10468764/
  • Date: 1999
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Prospective clinical study.
  • Population: 70 men aged 18–85 with community-acquired febrile UTI, fever, urinary symptoms/signs, and positive urine culture.
  • Patient relevance: Shows PSA can be very high during febrile urinary infection/prostatic involvement.
  • Main finding: 58/70 men (83%) had elevated PSA during acute illness; median PSA was 14 ng/mL. PSA often declined after one month, but some men had a protracted decrease.
  • Limitations: Febrile UTI population; older study; does not determine cancer risk after PSA normalization.
  • Questions for clinician: Was my PSA drawn during or soon after UTI/prostatitis, and what follow-up interval is appropriate?

docs.title: Prostatitis and serum prostate-specific antigen
docs.text:
• Citation: Current Urology Reports. 2005.
  • PMID: 15978235
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15978235/
  • Date: 2005
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Review.
  • Population: Men with different prostatitis categories.
  • Patient relevance: Explains mechanisms by which prostate inflammation can raise PSA.
  • Main finding: Acute prostatitis can increase PSA, usually returning toward normal after appropriate antibiotics within 1–3 months; chronic prostatitis has less predictable PSA changes.
  • Limitations: Review; older evidence base; management of asymptomatic elevated PSA remains nuanced.
  • Questions for clinician: Are my symptoms compatible with prostatitis, and is infection/inflammation being evaluated appropriately?

docs.title: Insight into infection-mediated prostate damage: contrasting patterns of CRP and PSA during infection
docs.text:
• Citation: Langston ME, et al. The Prostate. 2017.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1002/pros.23392; PMID 28703328
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28703328/
  • Date: 2017
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Case-control biomarker study.
  • Population: Young US military members with chlamydia, gonorrhea, non-chlamydial/non-gonococcal urethritis, infectious mononucleosis, other infections, and controls.
  • Patient relevance: Supports both direct prostate infection and indirect systemic inflammation mechanisms for PSA elevation.
  • Main finding: PSA and CRP patterns differed; findings supported possible direct and indirect mechanisms for infection-associated PSA elevation.
  • Limitations: Young military cohort; not a typical screening cohort.
  • Questions for clinician: Could recent infection or inflammation be relevant to this PSA result, and how should it be documented?

docs.title: Prostate-specific antigen, sexual behavior, and sexually transmitted infections in US men 40–59 years old, 2001–2004
docs.text:
• Citation: Sutcliffe S, et al. Infectious Agents and Cancer. 2007.
  • PMID: 17958890
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17958890/
  • Date: 2007
  • Category: Screening & Early Detection; Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Nationally representative cross-sectional study.
  • Population: US men aged 40–59.
  • Patient relevance: Provides a counterbalance to infection-positive studies by testing whether STI/sexual behavior measures broadly confound PSA in middle-aged men.
  • Main finding: No consistent association between sexual behavior/STI markers and PSA or percent-free PSA in the overall population.
  • Limitations: Cross-sectional; may miss recent/protracted infections or subgroups with higher exposure burden.
  • Questions for clinician: Does my individual recent infection history matter more than broad population averages?

docs.title: Influence of circumcision and sexual behaviour on PSA levels in patients attending an STD clinic
docs.text:
• Citation: Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2001.
  • PMID: 12497023
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12497023/
  • Date: 2001
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Retrospective serum/clinic data analysis.
  • Population: STD clinic attendees with anti-chlamydial antibody testing and PSA measurement.
  • Patient relevance: Suggests STI-related exposure markers may be associated with higher PSA in a high-exposure clinical population.
  • Main finding: Higher anti-chlamydial antibody titre was associated with a greater proportion of PSA >0.8 ng/mL; sexual behavior risk factors were associated with higher mean anti-chlamydial titre and PSA.
  • Limitations: Small, selected STD-clinic population; low PSA threshold; does not prove causation or cancer risk.
  • Questions for clinician: If I have a relevant STI history, should this be noted when interpreting PSA trends?

docs.title: The issue of prostate cancer evaluation in men with elevated PSA and chronic prostatitis
docs.text:
• Citation: Andrologia. 2008.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/j.1439-0272.2007.00820.x; PMID 18336465
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18336465/
  • Date: 2008
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Review.
  • Population: Men with elevated PSA and chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome.
  • Patient relevance: Frames the diagnostic problem: inflammation can elevate PSA, but undetected cancer remains possible.
  • Main finding: Chronic prostatitis/inflammation may raise PSA; antimicrobial treatment can lower PSA in some studies, but practical recommendations are lacking and missed cancer remains a concern.
  • Limitations: Review; evidence mixed; antibiotic response does not reliably exclude cancer.
  • Questions for clinician: If PSA falls after infection treatment, does it still need monitoring or further evaluation?

docs.title: Empiric antibiotics for an elevated PSA level: randomized prospective controlled multi-institutional trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Eggener SE, et al. BJU International. 2013.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/bju.12241; PMID 23890317
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23890317/
  • Date: 2013
  • Category: Diagnostic Workup; Guidelines & Care Pathways
  • Evidence type: Randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: Men with PSA >2.5 ng/mL, normal digital rectal examination, first prostate biopsy planned; men with recent UTI/prostatitis or clinical suspicion of UTI were excluded.
  • Patient relevance: Helps avoid unnecessary antibiotics for asymptomatic elevated PSA.
  • Main finding: Two weeks of ciprofloxacin did not significantly change PSA compared with no antibiotic; empiric antibiotics did not appear clinically beneficial for asymptomatic elevated PSA.
  • Limitations: Trial stopped early for futility/poor accrual; excludes men with actual suspected infection.
  • Questions for clinician: Do I have clinical evidence of infection that justifies antibiotics, or is the plan to repeat PSA/perform MRI/urology review instead?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• Was PSA measured during or soon after UTI, prostatitis, fever, STI, COVID/systemic infection, urinary retention, catheterization, cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, ejaculation, or vigorous cycling?
  • Were urinalysis and urine culture appropriate before interpreting the PSA?
  • If infection was likely or confirmed, when should PSA be repeated after symptoms resolve and treatment is completed?
  • What PSA level, PSA density, free PSA, MRI finding, digital rectal exam finding, or PSA trend would still trigger urology review or biopsy?
  • Is antibiotic treatment being used because there is evidence of infection, rather than solely to lower PSA?
  • If PSA normalizes, what monitoring interval is appropriate?
  • If PSA remains elevated, what is the next step: repeat PSA, MRI, risk calculator, urology referral, or biopsy?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
