This evidence should be read as a PSA interpretation and timing issue:

  1. UTI/prostatitis can strongly elevate PSA, sometimes with slow normalization.
  2. STIs and systemic infections may influence PSA, but population findings are mixed and context-dependent.
  3. Infection does not rule out cancer; it can coexist with cancer or obscure the signal.
  4. Empiric antibiotics are not a routine PSA strategy in asymptomatic men without infection evidence.
  5. The patient action is documentation and clinician discussion, not self-diagnosis or delay of evaluation.

docs.title: Nutrition and Prostate Cancer: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Category: Nutrition Scope: diet, dietary patterns, body composition, metabolic health, and selected supplements before diagnosis, after diagnosis, during treatment, and after treatment/survivorship.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes evidence for education and clinician discussion. It does not recommend any diet, supplement, fasting program, or treatment choice. Nutrition changes should be discussed with qualified clinicians, especially during active treatment, weight loss, diabetes management, anticoagulant use, kidney disease, frailty, or when supplements may interact with surgery, radiation, ADT, chemotherapy, or other medicines.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Phase Best-supported conclusion Strength of evidence Practical question for clinician Before diagnosis / incidence The strongest evidence is about overall dietary patterns rather than single foods. Western/inflammatory patterns and high dairy/calcium intake are associated with higher risk in some observational syntheses; healthy/plant-forward patterns may be associated with lower risk, but causality is uncertain. Moderate observational evidence; limited RCT evidence for prevention Should I focus on a heart-healthy, plant-forward diet rather than prostate-specific “anti-cancer” claims? After diagnosis / active surveillance Post-diagnosis plant-based, Mediterranean, and lower-inflammatory patterns are associated with lower progression risk in observational cohorts. RCTs show mixed results: increased vegetables alone did not reduce progression in MEAL, while an omega-3/low omega-6 intervention changed a biopsy proliferation biomarker in CAPFISH-3. Moderate observational evidence; limited and mixed RCT evidence Are there diet changes that support active surveillance without delaying appropriate treatment if risk changes? During treatment / ADT Diet and weight-management interventions may help cardiometabolic risk, weight, insulin resistance, and function during ADT, but evidence is smaller and not primarily about cancer control. Low-to-moderate interventional evidence for metabolic/supportive outcomes Should I have dietitian support for ADT-related weight, glucose, lipids, muscle, and cardiovascular risk? After treatment / survivorship Better diet quality and Mediterranean/plant-forward patterns are linked to better outcomes in some survivorship studies, but evidence remains mostly observational. The safest interpretation is general survivorship and cardiometabolic health support. Moderate observational evidence; limited prostate-specific intervention evidence What dietary pattern best supports recovery, cardiovascular health, weight, bowel function, and long-term survivorship?

docs.title: Nutrition topics covered
docs.text:
• Dietary patterns: Mediterranean, plant-based, healthy diet indices, Western/inflammatory patterns.
  • Single foods/nutrients: dairy/calcium, tomato sauce/lycopene, fish/omega-3 and omega-6 balance.
  • Active surveillance interventions: vegetable-rich diet counseling, omega-3/low omega-6 diet plus fish oil, weight-loss diet/exercise programs.
  • ADT/metabolic support: low-carbohydrate diet plus walking, diet/exercise behavior programs.
  • Survivorship diet quality: diet after diagnosis, progression, recurrence, cancer-specific mortality, and all-cause mortality.

docs.title: 1. Before diagnosis: incidence and risk of developing prostate cancer
docs.text:
The most patient-relevant message is that diet evidence is less definitive than claims often suggest. A 2025 systematic review of prospective cohorts and randomized trials found that among men without prostate cancer at baseline, plant-based and healthy dietary patterns were generally associated with lower total prostate cancer risk, while results varied across studies. A 2024 umbrella review emphasized that few modifiable risk factors are firmly established.

For specific dietary exposures, Western dietary patterns were associated with increased prostate cancer risk in a 2016 meta-analysis. Dairy and calcium showed modest positive associations with total prostate cancer risk in a 2015 cohort meta-analysis. Lycopene has long been studied, but a Cochrane review concluded evidence was insufficient for recommending lycopene supplementation for prostate cancer prevention.

Interpretation: a plant-forward, minimally processed, cardiometabolic-health diet is reasonable to discuss, but single-food or supplement claims should be treated cautiously.

docs.title: 2. After diagnosis: progression, recurrence, and mortality
docs.text:
Post-diagnosis evidence increasingly favors overall dietary patterns. A 2024 JAMA Network Open cohort found higher plant-based diet scores after diagnosis were associated with lower risk of disease progression among men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer. A 2021 active surveillance cohort found higher Mediterranean diet adherence was associated with lower risk of grade group progression.

Older Health Professionals Follow-Up Study data suggested post-diagnosis fish and tomato sauce intake were inversely associated with progression. More recent systematic reviews of post-diagnosis diet and lifestyle in prostate cancer survivors report that plant foods, healthier patterns, and minimizing inflammatory/hyperinsulinemic patterns are promising, but findings are not perfectly consistent.

Key caution: many post-diagnosis diet findings are observational. Men who eat healthier diets may also differ in healthcare access, exercise, weight, smoking, comorbidities, screening intensity, and treatment choices.

docs.title: 3. Active surveillance and treatment period
docs.text:
The best-known active surveillance RCT, MEAL, successfully increased vegetable intake but did not significantly reduce cancer progression over 24 months. This is important because it pushes against simplistic “eat more vegetables to stop progression” claims.

CAPFISH-3 tested a high omega-3, low omega-6 diet with fish oil in men on active surveillance and found a favorable change in Ki-67, a biopsy proliferation biomarker, over one year. This is promising but should not be overinterpreted as proof of better long-term cancer outcomes.

In overweight/obese men on active surveillance, PALS tested a structured diet and exercise weight-loss program and primarily targeted obesity/metabolic biomarkers rather than definitive prostate cancer outcomes. In men starting ADT, CAPS1 and IDEA-P suggest diet/lifestyle interventions can improve or support metabolic and functional outcomes, but these are supportive-care findings, not proven anti-cancer effects.

docs.title: 4. After treatment has ended: survivorship
docs.text:
Survivorship reviews suggest better overall diet quality and Mediterranean-style patterns may be associated with improved prognosis in cancer survivors, including prostate cancer, but prostate-specific intervention evidence remains limited. Nutrition after treatment should be framed around recovery, cardiometabolic health, bowel health, muscle preservation, weight stability or safe weight loss, and supporting tolerance of ongoing therapies if any.

Interpretation: after treatment, nutrition is best treated as survivorship support and risk-factor management rather than as a substitute for surveillance or clinician-directed treatment.

docs.title: Dietary Patterns in Prostate Cancer Prevention and Management
docs.text:
• Citation: Systematic review. European Urology. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2025.07.017; PMID 40835500
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40835500/
  • Date: 2025 Dec
  • Category: Nutrition; Screening & Early Detection; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of prospective cohort studies and randomized clinical trials.
  • Population: 63 studies: 49 cohort studies and 14 RCT reports.
  • Patient relevance: Broadest recent evidence map of diet patterns across prevention and management.
  • Main finding: Healthy, plant-based, Mediterranean, and lower-inflammatory patterns were generally more favorable; RCT evidence was more limited and less consistent.
  • Limitations: Dietary measurement heterogeneity, observational confounding, variable endpoints, and limited long-term RCT outcome data.
  • Questions for clinician: Which dietary pattern supports overall health and prostate cancer surveillance without overclaiming cancer-control benefit?

docs.title: Risk factors for prostate cancer: umbrella review of prospective observational studies and Mendelian randomization analyses
docs.text:
• Citation: PLOS Medicine. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1371/journal.pmed.1004362; PMID 38489391
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38489391/
  • Date: 2024 Mar
  • Category: Nutrition; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Umbrella review of prospective observational meta-analyses and Mendelian randomization analyses.
  • Population: Meta-analyses and MR studies of prostate cancer incidence risk factors.
  • Patient relevance: Helps separate plausible modifiable factors from less-certain associations.
  • Main finding: Few modifiable risk factors are firmly established; diet findings should be interpreted cautiously.
  • Limitations: Umbrella review depends on quality of underlying meta-analyses and MR assumptions.
  • Questions for clinician: Which diet claims are strong enough to affect personal decisions, and which are only hypothesis-generating?

docs.title: A Western Dietary Pattern Increases Prostate Cancer Risk
docs.text:
• Citation: Fabiani R, Minelli L, Bertarelli G, Bacci S. Nutrients. 2016.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3390/nu8100626; PMID 27754328
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27754328/
  • Date: 2016 Oct 12
  • Category: Nutrition; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of observational dietary-pattern studies.
  • Population: 12 observational studies.
  • Patient relevance: Addresses broad diet pattern rather than isolated nutrients.
  • Main finding: Western dietary pattern was associated with increased prostate cancer risk; “healthy pattern” was not clearly associated with lower risk.
  • Limitations: Observational studies, pattern definitions vary, residual confounding possible.
  • Questions for clinician: Should reducing Western-pattern foods be prioritized for general and prostate health?

docs.title: Dairy products, calcium, and prostate cancer risk
docs.text:
• Citation: Aune D, Navarro Rosenblatt DA, Chan DSM, et al. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2015.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3945/ajcn.113.067157; PMID 25527754
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25527754/
  • Date: 2015 Jan
  • Category: Nutrition; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of cohort studies.
  • Population: 32 prospective studies.
  • Patient relevance: Common question about dairy/calcium intake and prostate cancer risk.
  • Main finding: Higher total dairy, milk, low-fat milk, cheese, and dietary calcium intakes were modestly associated with increased total prostate cancer risk.
  • Limitations: Observational data, small effect sizes, confounding, and uncertainty about causality and individual dietary needs.
  • Questions for clinician: How should calcium, dairy, bone health, kidney stone risk, and prostate-cancer concerns be balanced?

docs.title: Lycopene for the prevention of prostate cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Ilic D, Forbes KM, Hassed C. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews. 2011.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1002/14651858.CD008007.pub2; PMID 22071840
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22071840/
  • Date: 2011 Nov 9
  • Category: Nutrition; Screening & Early Detection
  • Evidence type: Cochrane systematic review.
  • Population: Lycopene prevention studies.
  • Patient relevance: Evaluates a common tomato/lycopene supplement claim.
  • Main finding: Evidence was insufficient to support lycopene supplementation for prostate cancer prevention.
  • Limitations: Limited eligible trials, uncertainty about supplement vs food sources, older evidence base.
  • Questions for clinician: Is tomato-rich food reasonable as part of a healthy diet, while avoiding high-dose supplement claims?

docs.title: After diagnosis: progression, recurrence, and mortality
Diet after diagnosis and the risk of prostate cancer progression, recurrence, and death
docs.text:
• Citation: Chan JM, Holick CN, Leitzmann MF, et al. Cancer Causes & Control. 2006.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1007/s10552-005-0413-4; PMID 16425098
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16425098/
  • Date: 2006 Mar
  • Category: Nutrition; Active Surveillance; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Prospective cohort study after diagnosis.
  • Population: 1,202 men with localized/regional prostate cancer in the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study.
  • Patient relevance: Directly studies diet after diagnosis and progression outcomes.
  • Main finding: Higher fish and tomato sauce consumption were inversely associated with prostate cancer progression in this cohort.
  • Limitations: Observational, older treatment/surveillance context, self-reported diet, possible confounding.
  • Questions for clinician: Are fish and tomato-rich foods reasonable within a broader cardiometabolic diet plan?

docs.title: Plant-Based Diets and Disease Progression in Men With Prostate Cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Loeb S, et al. JAMA Network Open. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.9053; PMID 38691361
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38691361/
  • Date: 2024 May 1
  • Category: Nutrition; Active Surveillance; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Longitudinal observational cohort study.
  • Population: Men with nonmetastatic prostate cancer in the CaPSURE diet and lifestyle substudy.
  • Patient relevance: Modern postdiagnosis evidence on plant-based dietary patterns and progression.
  • Main finding: Higher postdiagnostic plant-based diet scores were associated with lower risk of prostate cancer progression.
  • Limitations: Observational, diet self-report, healthy-user confounding possible, not proof that diet prevents progression.
  • Questions for clinician: Would a plant-forward diet be appropriate given weight, protein needs, diabetes, bowel function, and treatment plan?

docs.title: Adherence to the Mediterranean diet and grade group progression in localized prostate cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Gregg JR, et al. Cancer. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1002/cncr.33182; PMID 33411364
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33411364/
  • Date: 2021 Mar 1
  • Category: Nutrition; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Prospective active-surveillance cohort.
  • Population: 410 men with newly diagnosed prostate cancer on active surveillance.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant to men choosing active surveillance.
  • Main finding: Higher Mediterranean diet adherence was associated with lower risk of grade group progression.
  • Limitations: Observational, single cohort, progression definitions and follow-up duration matter.
  • Questions for clinician: Is a Mediterranean-style diet appropriate during active surveillance, and what monitoring remains essential?

docs.title: Effect of a behavioral intervention to increase vegetable consumption: MEAL randomized clinical trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Parsons JK, et al. JAMA. 2020.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1001/jama.2019.20207; PMID 31935026
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31935026/
  • Date: 2020 Jan 14
  • Category: Nutrition; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Randomized clinical trial.
  • Population: 478 men aged 50–80 with early-stage prostate cancer on active surveillance.
  • Patient relevance: High-quality test of a vegetable-rich diet intervention in early-stage prostate cancer.
  • Main finding: The intervention increased vegetable intake but did not significantly reduce cancer progression over 24 months.
  • Limitations: Single dietary target, 24-month duration, early-stage population, may not test all healthy dietary patterns.
  • Questions for clinician: What should be concluded from a negative RCT, and should diet goals focus on overall health rather than cancer control alone?

docs.title: High omega-3, low omega-6 diet with fish oil for men on active surveillance: CAPFISH-3
docs.text:
• Citation: Aronson WJ, et al. Journal of Clinical Oncology. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1200/JCO.24.00608; PMID 39671538
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39671538/
  • Date: 2025 Mar
  • Category: Nutrition; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Phase II randomized clinical trial.
  • Population: 100 men with Grade Group 1 or 2 prostate cancer on active surveillance.
  • Patient relevance: Tests a specific diet plus fish oil strategy in active surveillance.
  • Main finding: The intervention produced a favorable change in Ki-67 proliferation index over one year compared with control.
  • Limitations: Biomarker endpoint, relatively small trial, not proof of lower metastasis, mortality, or long-term progression.
  • Questions for clinician: Are fish oil capsules appropriate or risky given bleeding risk, medications, cardiovascular history, and treatment plans?

docs.title: Treatment period, ADT, and metabolic support
PALS: diet and exercise in overweight and obese men on active surveillance
docs.text:
• Citation: Prostate Cancer Active Lifestyle Study. Cancer. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1002/cncr.35241; PMID 38353455
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38353455/
  • Date: 2024 Jun 15
  • Category: Nutrition; Exercise; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: Overweight/obese men with prostate cancer on active surveillance.
  • Patient relevance: Tests a practical lifestyle program targeting weight and metabolic biomarkers.
  • Main finding: Structured diet and exercise achieved weight loss and improved selected obesity/metabolic biomarkers; the main focus was not definitive cancer-control outcomes.
  • Limitations: Combined diet/exercise intervention, short duration, biomarker-focused endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: Would safe weight loss improve metabolic health without risking muscle loss or treatment readiness?

docs.title: Low-carbohydrate diet plus walking during ADT: CAPS1 randomized controlled trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Freedland SJ, et al. Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1038/s41391-019-0126-5; PMID 30664736
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30664736/
  • Date: 2019 Sep
  • Category: Nutrition; Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: Men initiating ADT.
  • Patient relevance: ADT can worsen insulin resistance, weight, lipids, and body composition.
  • Main finding: Low-carbohydrate diet plus walking improved weight and some metabolic markers, but trial stopped early and primary endpoint was not definitively met at 6 months.
  • Limitations: Small sample due to early stopping, combined diet/exercise intervention, metabolic rather than cancer-control endpoints.
  • Questions for clinician: Is carbohydrate restriction safe and appropriate given diabetes medications, weight, kidney function, and cardiovascular risk?

docs.title: Group-mediated exercise and dietary intervention during ADT: IDEA-P
docs.text:
• Citation: Focht BC, et al. Annals of Behavioral Medicine. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1093/abm/kax002; PMID 29684136
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29684136/
  • Date: 2018 Apr 19
  • Category: Nutrition; Exercise; Advanced & Recurrent Disease; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Pilot randomized trial.
  • Population: 32 men undergoing ADT.
  • Patient relevance: Tests a behavioral diet/exercise support model during ADT.
  • Main finding: Feasible with favorable adherence and no serious intervention-related adverse events; suggested benefits for mobility, body composition, and strength.
  • Limitations: Pilot size, short follow-up, combined diet and exercise, not powered for cancer outcomes.
  • Questions for clinician: Would a structured group or coaching program improve adherence during ADT?

docs.title: Effect of postdiagnosis diet and lifestyle on clinical outcomes in prostate cancer survivors
docs.text:
• Citation: Systematic review. Journal of Urology. 2026.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000004855; PMID 41270186
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41270186/
  • Date: 2026 Apr
  • Category: Nutrition; Side Effects & Quality of Life; Active Surveillance
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of clinical trials and cohort studies.
  • Population: Men diagnosed with prostate cancer; studies of progression, recurrence, prostate-cancer-specific mortality, PSA kinetics, and related outcomes.
  • Patient relevance: Most directly aligned with survivorship and secondary prevention decisions.
  • Main finding: Several studies suggest plant foods, healthier dietary/lifestyle patterns, and minimizing inflammatory or hyperinsulinemic foods may be associated with better outcomes, but not all studies agree.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneous exposures and outcomes; mixture of observational and trial evidence; conclusions are cautious.
  • Questions for clinician: Which diet and lifestyle targets are sensible for survivorship without implying guaranteed recurrence prevention?

docs.title: The Role of Diet in Prognosis among Cancer Survivors
docs.text:
• Citation: Castro-Espin C, Agudo A. Nutrients. 2022.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3390/nu14020348; PMID 35057525
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35057525/
  • Date: 2022 Jan 14
  • Category: Nutrition; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of dietary patterns and diet interventions in cancer survivors.
  • Population: 35 prospective cohort studies and 14 RCTs across cancer survivor populations, including prostate cancer.
  • Patient relevance: Places prostate cancer survivorship in broader cancer nutrition evidence.
  • Main finding: Better overall diet quality was associated with improved survival in several survivor groups; Mediterranean diet adherence was associated with lower mortality risk in colorectal and prostate cancer survivors.
  • Limitations: Multi-cancer evidence, observational dominance, heterogeneity, and limited prostate-specific RCTs.
  • Questions for clinician: Should survivorship nutrition follow a Mediterranean-style, high-quality diet pattern unless contraindicated?

docs.title: The Facts about Food after Cancer Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Citation: Schwedhelm C, et al. Nutrients. 2020.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.3390/nu12082345; PMID 32764484
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32764484/
  • Date: 2020 Aug 5
  • Category: Nutrition; Side Effects & Quality of Life
  • Evidence type: Systematic review of prospective cohort studies after cancer diagnosis.
  • Population: 29 prospective studies, including 8 prostate cancer studies.
  • Patient relevance: Addresses common post-diagnosis diet questions across survivorship.
  • Main finding: Healthier diet quality, vegetables/fiber, and high-quality protein appeared beneficial; Western diet and high saturated fat were associated with worse outcomes in some studies.
  • Limitations: Observational evidence, multi-cancer synthesis, risk of self-made/web-based diet misuse.
  • Questions for clinician: How can diet support adequate protein, muscle, bowel health, and cardiometabolic risk after treatment?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• Which phase am I in: screening/risk reduction, active surveillance, surgery/radiation, ADT/systemic therapy, or survivorship?
  • Do I need referral to an oncology dietitian, especially if losing weight, gaining weight on ADT, diabetic, frail, or using supplements?
  • Are there foods/supplements that could interact with anticoagulants, surgery, radiation side effects, ADT, chemotherapy, kidney disease, or diabetes medications?
  • Should the focus be Mediterranean-style, plant-forward, high-fiber diet quality rather than single supplements?
  • How should calcium and dairy decisions balance prostate-cancer uncertainty with bone health, especially if on ADT?
  • If considering fish oil or omega-3 supplements, what are the bleeding, medication, and cardiovascular considerations?
  • What outcomes should be tracked: weight, waist, muscle/strength, lipids, glucose/HbA1c, blood pressure, bowel symptoms, fatigue, and treatment tolerance?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
This evidence strengthens the Nutrition category by adding a structured distinction between:

  1. Incidence prevention claims — diet pattern evidence is suggestive but not definitive; avoid single-food/supplement certainty.
  2. Post-diagnosis associations — plant-forward, Mediterranean, and low-inflammatory patterns look promising but are mostly observational.
  3. Active surveillance trials — increased vegetables alone did not reduce progression in MEAL; omega-3/low omega-6 diet changed a biomarker in CAPFISH-3 but needs longer-term clinical outcomes.
  4. Treatment/survivorship support — nutrition is strongest as cardiometabolic, weight, muscle, bowel, and quality-of-life support, especially during ADT and survivorship.

docs.title: PSMA PET/CT in Prostate Cancer Diagnosis, Staging, Recurrence, and Treatment: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04

Category: Imaging & biomarkers; risk stratification & staging; advanced & recurrent disease Scope: Patient-facing synthesis of where prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) PET/CT fits in the prostate-cancer journey: initial diagnostic work-up, staging before curative-intent treatment, biochemical recurrence after treatment, selection for PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy, and selected issues in treatment monitoring.

Not medical advice

This page is for evidence review and clinician discussion. PSMA PET/CT can change staging and treatment plans, but whether to order it, how to interpret it, and what treatment should follow depends on the full clinical context, local availability, tracer used, prior treatments, pathology, PSA kinetics, symptoms, kidney function, radiation exposure considerations, and specialist judgement.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Patient question Evidence-supported answer Clinician discussion question Can PSMA PET/CT diagnose prostate cancer by itself? Not usually. MRI and biopsy remain the core diagnostic pathway. PSMA PET/CT can show suspicious intraprostatic uptake and may help target biopsy in selected settings, but specificity is imperfect and a tissue diagnosis is still central. "Would PSMA PET add information beyond mpMRI and biopsy in my case, or would it risk confusing the diagnostic pathway?" When is PSMA PET/CT strongest before treatment? Strongest evidence is for staging higher-risk localized or locally advanced prostate cancer before curative-intent surgery or radiotherapy. The randomized proPSMA trial found PSMA PET/CT more accurate than CT plus bone scan. "Given my PSA, Grade Group, MRI stage, and risk category, am I in the group where PSMA PET/CT is likely to change staging or the treatment field?" What if PSA rises after surgery or radiation? PSMA PET/CT is a major tool for localizing biochemical recurrence, often detecting disease when conventional imaging is negative. Detection increases with PSA level, but useful findings can occur at relatively low PSA. "At my current PSA and PSA doubling time, would a PSMA PET result change salvage radiation, metastasis-directed therapy, systemic therapy, or surveillance?" Can a negative PSMA PET/CT rule out disease? No. Microscopic disease can be below PET resolution or have low PSMA expression. A negative scan may still support treatment such as early salvage radiotherapy if other risk factors indicate it. "If my scan is negative, what treatment or monitoring would still be recommended based on pathology and PSA kinetics?" Why does PSMA PET matter for advanced disease? It can identify whether tumors express enough PSMA to consider PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy such as lutetium-177 PSMA-617 in selected metastatic castration-resistant settings. "Does my disease meet imaging and clinical eligibility for PSMA-targeted therapy, and do we need FDG PET to look for PSMA-negative aggressive disease?" Can PSMA PET/CT be used to monitor ongoing treatment? It may help clarify response or progression in some advanced-disease settings, but routine monitoring schedules are not as standardized as PSA, symptoms, CT/bone imaging, and treatment-specific assessments. "What result on PSMA PET would actually change my current treatment plan, and what are the alternatives for monitoring?"

docs.title: Where PSMA PET/CT fits in the care pathway
1. Initial diagnosis: promising adjunct, not a biopsy replacement
docs.text:
PSMA PET/CT detects cellular expression of PSMA, which is commonly high in prostate cancer, but uptake is not perfectly cancer-specific and not all clinically important cancer is equally PSMA-avid. Current diagnostic pathways still rely on PSA context, digital rectal examination where relevant, mpMRI, and prostate biopsy.

The EAU diagnostic-evaluation guideline notes that radiolabelled PSMA PET imaging is mainly used for staging, while it may also help indicate precise locations for targeted biopsies or follow selected active-surveillance candidates. It reports pooled performance for clinically significant prostate cancer detection and describes trials combining PSMA PET with MRI, but this remains an adjunctive role rather than routine first-line diagnosis for every patient.[^eau-diagnostic]

Patient interpretation: PSMA PET can sharpen the question "where is disease?" but it does not usually answer "do I have prostate cancer?" without pathology.

docs.title: 2. Initial staging before curative-intent treatment: strongest diagnostic evidence
docs.text:
The strongest randomized evidence is in men with high-risk localized prostate cancer being staged before curative-intent surgery or radiotherapy.

The Australian proPSMA randomized trial compared first-line gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT with conventional CT plus bone scan in high-risk localized prostate cancer. PSMA PET/CT had substantially better accuracy for pelvic nodal or distant metastatic disease:

  • accuracy: 92% vs 65% for conventional imaging;
  • sensitivity: 85% vs 38%;
  • specificity: 98% vs 91%;
  • fewer equivocal findings: 7% vs 23%;
  • more management changes as first-line imaging: 28% vs 15%.[^propsma]

The OSPREY phase 2/3 trial of fluorine-18 DCFPyL PET/CT found very high specificity for pelvic nodal disease in high-risk patients undergoing prostatectomy and lymphadenectomy, but sensitivity was limited:

  • median specificity: 97.9%;
  • median sensitivity: 40.3%;
  • median positive predictive value: 86.7%.[^osprey]

Patient interpretation: a positive PSMA-avid lymph node or distant lesion is often meaningful and may change the treatment plan, but a negative scan does not exclude microscopic nodal disease. Surgical pathology, radiation-field decisions, and systemic-treatment decisions may still depend on risk factors beyond the scan.

docs.title: 3. Biochemical recurrence after surgery or radiation: high clinical utility, but not perfect
docs.text:
When PSA rises after definitive treatment, the key practical question is whether disease is local, nodal, bone/visceral metastatic, or not yet visible. PSMA PET/CT is now one of the most important imaging tools for this situation.

The CONDOR phase 3 trial studied fluorine-18 DCFPyL PET/CT in men with biochemical recurrence and uninformative standard imaging. Median baseline PSA was 0.8 ng/mL. It found:

  • correct localization rate: 84.8%–87.0% across readers;
  • disease detection rate: 59%–66%;
  • intended management changed in 63.9% of evaluable patients.[^condor]

A histopathology-validated meta-analysis of gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET reported high positive predictive value in recurrence and detection that rose with PSA:

  • detection rate 63% when PSA was less than 2.0 ng/mL;
  • detection rate 94% when PSA was more than 2.0 ng/mL.[^hope-meta]

A prospective Australian multicenter study found PSMA PET/CT changed intended management in 51% overall and 62% among patients with biochemical failure after surgery or radiation.[^roach]

Patient interpretation: PSMA PET/CT is especially useful when the result will change a salvage plan: prostate-bed radiation alone vs pelvic nodal treatment, metastasis-directed therapy, systemic therapy, clinical trial, or observation. A negative scan can still be compatible with microscopic recurrence.

docs.title: 4. Relationship to ongoing treatment and treatment monitoring
docs.text:
PSMA PET/CT has several treatment-related roles, but these should be separated:

  1. Planning curative-intent local treatment: before surgery or radiotherapy in higher-risk disease, it can reveal nodal or distant disease that changes local vs systemic strategy.
  2. Planning salvage treatment: after PSA recurrence, it can localize recurrence and help shape salvage radiation fields or metastasis-directed therapy.
  3. Selecting patients for PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy: in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, PSMA PET can identify whether lesions are sufficiently PSMA-positive for therapy such as lutetium-177 PSMA-617.
  4. Monitoring response/progression: quantitative PSMA PET measures are being studied and may help in selected advanced-disease decisions, but routine monitoring is less standardized than its staging and recurrence-localization roles.

The key patient question is not simply "can we scan?" but "what decision would change if the scan is positive, negative, mixed, or shows new sites?"

docs.title: 5. Advanced disease and theranostics: imaging linked to treatment eligibility
docs.text:
PSMA PET/CT becomes part of a theranostic pathway when the imaging target can also be treated. In metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, trials of lutetium-177 PSMA-617 required PSMA-positive imaging, and some protocols also used FDG PET to exclude aggressive PSMA-negative/FDG-positive discordant disease.

The phase 3 VISION trial enrolled patients with PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer previously treated with androgen-receptor pathway inhibition and taxane chemotherapy. Adding lutetium-177 PSMA-617 to standard care improved:

  • imaging-based progression-free survival: 8.7 vs 3.4 months;
  • overall survival: 15.3 vs 11.3 months.[^vision]

The randomized phase 2 TheraP trial compared lutetium-177 PSMA-617 with cabazitaxel in men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer after docetaxel, using PSMA PET and FDG PET selection. It found:

  • PSA50 responses: 66% vs 37% with cabazitaxel;
  • grade 3–4 adverse events: 33% vs 53% with cabazitaxel.[^therap]

With longer follow-up, TheraP did not show an overall-survival difference between randomized groups, supporting lutetium-177 PSMA-617 as an alternative rather than proving superiority over cabazitaxel for survival in that trial.[^therap-os]

The later PSMAfore trial, in taxane-naive PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer after progression on one androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor, reported improved radiographic progression-free survival in earlier publications; a 2025 patient-reported-outcomes analysis found delayed worsening of quality of life, pain, and symptomatic skeletal events with lutetium-177 PSMA-617 compared with ARPI change.[^psmafore]

Patient interpretation: PSMA PET is not just a map in advanced disease; it can be a treatment-selection test. But eligibility depends on cancer stage, prior treatments, blood counts, kidney function, symptoms, whole-body disease pattern, PSMA expression, possible PSMA-negative lesions, and drug availability/reimbursement.

docs.title: Practical limitations and pitfalls
docs.text: Pitfall Why it matters Stage migration PSMA PET finds disease that older imaging misses. This can improve targeting, but it also makes outcomes hard to compare with older studies staged by CT/bone scan. False positives Benign ganglia, fractures, inflammation, healing bone lesions, and non-prostate malignancies can sometimes show uptake. Correlation with CT/MRI, pattern, history, and sometimes biopsy is needed. False negatives Small-volume disease may be below resolution; some aggressive or dedifferentiated prostate cancer may be less PSMA-avid. Tracer differences Gallium-68 PSMA-11, fluorine-18 DCFPyL, fluorine-18 PSMA-1007, and other tracers differ in urinary excretion, biodistribution, and false-positive patterns. Access and reimbursement Availability and indication rules differ by country, scanner access, tracer supply, and insurer/public-system criteria. A scan is not a treatment plan The best use of PSMA PET is when the care team has pre-specified what would change based on each possible result.

docs.title: Hofman et al. 2020 — proPSMA randomized trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Hofman MS, et al. Lancet. 2020.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(20)30314-7; PMID 32209449
  • Evidence type: Prospective randomized multicenter diagnostic trial.
  • Population: Men with high-risk localized prostate cancer before curative-intent surgery or radiotherapy.
  • Patient relevance: Best evidence that PSMA PET/CT outperforms CT plus bone scan for initial staging in higher-risk disease.
  • Main finding: Accuracy 92% vs 65%; management change 28% vs 15%; equivocal findings 7% vs 23%.
  • Limitations: Staging accuracy and management changes do not automatically prove improved long-term survival; results apply mainly to high-risk staging.
  • Questions for clinician: "Would this result alter surgery, lymph-node dissection, radiation fields, ADT/systemic therapy, or trial eligibility?"

docs.title: Pienta et al. 2021 — OSPREY phase 2/3 trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Pienta KJ, et al. Journal of Urology. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000001698; PMID 33634707
  • Evidence type: Prospective multicenter diagnostic trial of fluorine-18 DCFPyL PET/CT.
  • Population: High-risk primary staging cohort plus suspected recurrent/metastatic cohort.
  • Patient relevance: Supports high specificity/positive predictive value of PSMA-avid lesions, but highlights limited sensitivity for pelvic nodal micrometastases.
  • Main finding: Pelvic nodal specificity 97.9%; sensitivity 40.3%; PPV 86.7%.
  • Limitations: A negative scan cannot reliably exclude microscopic pelvic nodal disease.
  • Questions for clinician: "If my PSMA PET is negative, how will you still estimate microscopic nodal risk?"

docs.title: Morris et al. 2021 — CONDOR phase 3 trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Morris MJ, et al. Clinical Cancer Research. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1158/1078-0432.CCR-20-4573; PMID 33622706
  • Evidence type: Prospective phase 3 multicenter diagnostic trial.
  • Population: Biochemical recurrence with uninformative standard imaging.
  • Patient relevance: Directly relevant to a rising PSA after treatment.
  • Main finding: Correct localization 84.8%–87.0%; disease detection 59%–66%; intended management changed in 63.9%.
  • Limitations: Management change is not the same as proven improvement in survival or quality of life.
  • Questions for clinician: "What salvage or systemic treatment decision depends on localizing recurrence now?"

docs.title: Hope et al. 2019 — histopathology-validated meta-analysis
docs.text:
• Citation: Hope TA, et al. Journal of Nuclear Medicine. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.2967/jnumed.118.219501; PMID 30530831
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET accuracy.
  • Population: Initial staging and biochemical recurrence studies with pathology correlation where available.
  • Patient relevance: Helps explain why PSA level affects the chance of seeing recurrent disease.
  • Main finding: Initial-staging pelvic nodal sensitivity 0.74 and specificity 0.96 in pooled pathology-correlated studies; biochemical-recurrence detection 63% when PSA <2.0 and 94% when PSA >2.0.
  • Limitations: Heterogeneity across studies, tracers, scanners, patient groups, and reference standards.
  • Questions for clinician: "At my PSA, what is the realistic chance of a positive scan and what would we do with each result?"

docs.title: Roach et al. 2018 — management-intent study
docs.text:
• Citation: Roach PJ, et al. Journal of Nuclear Medicine. 2018.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.2967/jnumed.117.197160; PMID 28646014
  • Evidence type: Prospective multicenter Australian management-impact study.
  • Population: Primary and recurrent prostate cancer patients referred for PSMA PET/CT.
  • Patient relevance: Shows how often clinicians changed intended management after PSMA PET/CT.
  • Main finding: Management intent changed in 51% overall and 62% after biochemical failure.
  • Limitations: Change in planned management does not by itself establish better outcomes.
  • Questions for clinician: "Would this scan change management in a way that is evidence-based and acceptable to me?"

docs.title: Sartor et al. 2021 — VISION trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Sartor O, et al. New England Journal of Medicine. 2021.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2107322; PMID 34161051
  • Evidence type: Phase 3 randomized treatment trial.
  • Population: PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer after androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor and taxane treatment.
  • Patient relevance: Shows how PSMA imaging can select patients for PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy.
  • Main finding: Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 plus standard care improved imaging-based progression-free survival and overall survival.
  • Limitations: Applies to selected advanced disease, not early localized prostate cancer; treatment has hematologic, renal, salivary, fatigue, nausea, and other safety considerations.
  • Questions for clinician: "Do my scan pattern, prior treatments, symptoms, blood counts, and kidney function make me eligible?"

docs.title: Hofman et al. 2021 / 2024 — TheraP trial
docs.text:
• Citation: Hofman MS, et al. Lancet. 2021; mature OS update in Lancet Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(21)00237-3, PMID 33581798; DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(23)00529-6, PMID 38043558
  • Evidence type: Randomized phase 2 treatment trial.
  • Population: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer after docetaxel, eligible for cabazitaxel; selected with PSMA PET and FDG PET.
  • Patient relevance: Illustrates why PSMA positivity and absence of discordant FDG-positive/PSMA-negative disease matter.
  • Main finding: Higher PSA50 response and fewer grade 3–4 adverse events than cabazitaxel; no overall-survival difference on mature follow-up.
  • Limitations: Phase 2 design; selection criteria matter; cross-over and subsequent therapies complicate OS interpretation.
  • Questions for clinician: "Would FDG PET be useful to identify aggressive PSMA-negative disease before PSMA-targeted therapy?"

docs.title: EAU guideline diagnostic-evaluation section
docs.text:
• Source: European Association of Urology prostate cancer guideline, diagnostic evaluation chapter.
  • URL: https://uroweb.org/guidelines/prostate-cancer/chapter/diagnostic-evaluation
  • Evidence type: Guideline synthesis.
  • Patient relevance: Places PSMA PET/CT in context: mainly staging, with selected adjunctive diagnostic roles.
  • Main finding: PSMA PET is mainly used for staging; mpMRI remains central for primary diagnosis; PSMA PET + MRI may improve sensitivity/NPV in selected diagnostic studies but can reduce specificity.
  • Limitations: Guideline recommendations change over time and vary by region; local availability and reimbursement matter.
  • Questions for clinician: "Which guideline or local policy are you using for PSMA PET in my situation?"

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
Bring these questions to the treating urologist, radiation oncologist, medical oncologist, or nuclear medicine physician:

  • Indication: Is the scan for initial staging, recurrence localization, treatment selection, or monitoring?
  • Decision impact: What exact decision would change if the scan is positive, negative, equivocal, or shows distant disease?
  • Risk group: Does my Grade Group, PSA, PSA density, MRI stage, clinical stage, or PSA doubling time place me in a group where PSMA PET is evidence-supported?
  • Tracer: Which tracer is available locally, and what are its common pitfalls?
  • Negative scan: If no disease is seen, will that change treatment or only indicate disease is below detection?
  • Equivocal lesion: Would follow-up MRI/CT, repeat imaging, biopsy, or multidisciplinary review be needed?
  • Radiation planning: If recurrence is found, would the scan alter prostate-bed, pelvic-node, or metastasis-directed radiation fields?
  • Advanced disease: If considering lutetium-177 PSMA therapy, do we need FDG PET or other imaging to detect PSMA-negative disease?
  • Monitoring: How often would imaging be repeated, and would PSA/symptoms/CT/bone scan be more appropriate for routine follow-up?
  • Access/cost: Is this scan reimbursed for my indication, and how quickly can it be performed without delaying necessary treatment?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
For this dashboard, PSMA PET/CT should be treated as a decision-changing imaging tool, not as a stand-alone diagnostic answer. The evidence is strongest for:

  1. staging higher-risk disease before curative-intent treatment;
  2. localizing biochemical recurrence when the result will guide salvage or systemic treatment;
  3. selecting eligible advanced-disease patients for PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy.

The main uncertainty is not whether PSMA PET/CT sees more disease than older imaging—it often does—but whether each scan-driven management change improves outcomes for the individual patient. That makes the pre-scan clinical question essential.

[^eau-diagnostic]: European Association of Urology. Prostate Cancer Guidelines, Diagnostic Evaluation chapter. https://uroweb.org/guidelines/prostate-cancer/chapter/diagnostic-evaluation [^propsma]: Hofman MS, et al. Prostate-specific membrane antigen PET-CT in patients with high-risk prostate cancer before curative-intent surgery or radiotherapy (proPSMA). Lancet. 2020. PMID: 32209449. [^osprey]: Pienta KJ, et al. A Phase 2/3 prospective multicenter study of diagnostic accuracy of PSMA PET/CT with 18F-DCFPyL in prostate cancer patients (OSPREY). J Urol. 2021. PMID: 33634707. [^condor]: Morris MJ, et al. Diagnostic performance of 18F-DCFPyL-PET/CT in men with biochemically recurrent prostate cancer (CONDOR). Clin Cancer Res. 2021. PMID: 33622706. [^hope-meta]: Hope TA, et al. Metaanalysis of 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET accuracy for the detection of prostate cancer validated by histopathology. J Nucl Med. 2019. PMID: 30530831. [^roach]: Roach PJ, et al. The impact of 68Ga-PSMA PET/CT on management intent in prostate cancer. J Nucl Med. 2018. PMID: 28646014. [^vision]: Sartor O, et al. Lutetium-177-PSMA-617 for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. N Engl J Med. 2021. PMID: 34161051. [^therap]: Hofman MS, et al. 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus cabazitaxel in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (TheraP). Lancet. 2021. PMID: 33581798. [^therap-os]: Hofman MS, et al. Overall survival with 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus cabazitaxel in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (TheraP). Lancet Oncology. 2024. PMID: 38043558. [^psmafore]: Morris MJ, et al./PSMAfore investigators. Health-related quality of life, pain, and symptomatic skeletal events with 177Lu-PSMA-617 in progressive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (PSMAfore). Lancet Oncology. 2025. PMID: 40441170.

docs.title: Transperineal vs Transrectal Prostate Biopsy: Evidence Map
docs.text:
Retrieved: 2026-07-04 Categories: Diagnostic Workup; Screening & Early Detection; Guidelines & Care Pathways Scope: prostate biopsy route choice, infection/sepsis risk, cancer detection, pain/tolerability, anesthesia, antibiotic stewardship, circumstances where each route is used, and trends over time.

Not medical advice

This page summarizes evidence for education and clinician discussion. It does not decide whether a biopsy is needed or which route is best for an individual patient. Biopsy decisions depend on PSA pattern, MRI findings, DRE, prostate size/location of lesions, infection risk, anticoagulants, anatomy, available local expertise, anesthesia options, and patient preference.

docs.title: Patient-facing bottom line
docs.text: Question Current evidence-based answer Practical clinician question Which route is safer for infection? Transperineal biopsy generally has lower infection/sepsis risk because the needle avoids passing through rectal mucosa. Modern transrectal biopsy can be safer when rectal swab-targeted antibiotics and rectal antisepsis are used, but it still depends on antibiotic stewardship and local resistance patterns. What is this centre’s infection/sepsis rate for each approach, and what prophylaxis is used? Which route finds more clinically significant cancer? Recent randomized and prospective evidence suggests broadly similar detection in many settings; TRANSLATE found higher Grade Group ≥2 detection with local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy, while PREVENT/ProBE-PC and meta-analyses often show similar clinically significant cancer detection. Transperineal access may help sample anterior/apical regions. Where is the MRI lesion—anterior, apical, posterior, transition zone—and does route affect access? Which is more painful or awkward? Transperineal biopsy under local anesthesia is feasible and increasingly office-based, but several studies report slightly more immediate pain or embarrassment. Differences are usually small and short-lived when local anesthesia is done well. How will local anesthesia be delivered, and what pain-control options are available? Which is most common now? Historically, transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy was the standard. The trend is toward greater transperineal use, especially in Europe/UK/Australia-style pathways, because of infection concerns, antibiotic resistance, MRI-targeted biopsy, and the rise of local-anesthetic/freehand transperineal techniques. Is this practice still mainly transrectal, or has it transitioned to transperineal? Why?

docs.title: What the two approaches mean
docs.text: Approach Needle path Typical setting Main advantages Main trade-offs Transrectal biopsy (TR/TRUS) Needle passes through rectal wall into prostate Historically office-based under local anesthesia; still common in many centres Familiar, fast, widely available, simpler positioning/equipment in established practices Higher infection/sepsis concern because rectal bacteria can be introduced; requires antibiotic prophylaxis and often rectal antisepsis/culture-based targeting Transperineal biopsy (TP) Needle passes through cleaned perineal skin between scrotum and anus Previously often general anesthesia/template grid; now increasingly local-anesthetic office/freehand or MRI-fusion targeted Lower infectious risk, better antibiotic stewardship, good anterior/apical access, avoids rectal wall May require lithotomy positioning, perineal local anesthesia, different equipment/training; can be more immediately uncomfortable/embarrassing

docs.title: Circumstances where each route may be used
Situations that often favor transperineal biopsy
docs.text:
• Higher concern about infection or antimicrobial resistance.
  • Prior post-biopsy infection, resistant rectal flora, recurrent UTIs, diabetes/immunosuppression, or other infection-risk considerations.
  • Need to reduce or avoid antibiotic exposure where clinically appropriate.
  • MRI lesions in anterior, apical, or transition-zone locations that may be easier to sample via a perineal route.
  • Repeat biopsy after prior negative transrectal biopsy but persistent suspicion.
  • Local centre has strong office-based local-anesthetic transperineal expertise.
  • Patient preference to avoid rectal needle passage.

docs.title: Situations where transrectal biopsy may still be used
docs.text:
• Local transperineal service is unavailable or waiting time is substantially longer.
  • Patient cannot tolerate lithotomy positioning or perineal access.
  • The centre has a well-established transrectal pathway with targeted antibiotic prophylaxis, rectal preparation, and low documented infection rates.
  • Posterior/peripheral-zone target is straightforward and operator expertise is strongest transrectally.
  • Specific anatomy, equipment, sedation, or procedural constraints make transrectal approach more practical.

docs.title: Situations requiring individualized planning either way
docs.text:
• Anticoagulants/antiplatelets or bleeding risk.
  • Prior rectal surgery, absent rectum, inflammatory bowel disease, anal/rectal pathology.
  • Severe urinary symptoms or high urinary-retention risk.
  • Large prostate, small/anterior MRI lesions, or prior negative biopsy.
  • Need for systematic plus MRI-targeted sampling.
  • Anxiety, pain tolerance, sedation preference, and local anesthesia plan.

docs.title: Trends over time
docs.text:
1. Traditional era: transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy became the dominant office procedure because it was technically simple and widely available.
  2. Infection concern era: increasing fluoroquinolone resistance and post-biopsy sepsis concerns led to targeted prophylaxis, augmented antibiotics, rectal povidone-iodine preparation, and renewed interest in transperineal biopsy.
  3. MRI-targeted era: pre-biopsy MRI and fusion/cognitive targeting changed sampling strategy; route choice became tied to lesion location, targeted plus systematic cores, and local technology.
  4. Modern transition: local-anesthetic, freehand, office-based transperineal biopsy reduced the historical need for general anesthesia/template-grid operating-theatre biopsy.
  5. Guideline direction: European guidance now strongly favors transperineal biopsy because of low infectious risk and antibiotic stewardship. Some other systems still allow either route, especially where transrectal infection prevention is optimized.
  6. Current nuance: recent RCTs show transperineal’s infection advantage may be smaller when transrectal biopsy uses targeted prophylaxis and rectal preparation. The direction of travel still favors transperineal adoption, but transrectal biopsy remains clinically viable in well-run pathways.

docs.title: Infection and sepsis
docs.text:
The central safety concern is that transrectal biopsy traverses rectal mucosa. EAU guideline text reports higher infectious complications after transrectal biopsy than transperineal biopsy in pooled randomized data, sepsis rates of 0.1% for transperineal versus 0.9% for transrectal biopsy in a large systematic review, and lower UK readmission rates for sepsis after transperineal versus transrectal biopsy.

However, modern RCTs complicate a simple “TP always safer” message. PREVENT found 0 infections in transperineal biopsy without antibiotics versus 1.4% after transrectal biopsy with rectal-swab-targeted prophylaxis; the difference did not reach conventional statistical significance. ProBE-PC found similar composite infectious complication rates between routes when performed in office settings.

Interpretation: transperineal biopsy is favored for infection prevention and antibiotic stewardship, but high-quality transrectal pathways can achieve low infection rates.

docs.title: Cancer detection
docs.text:
For clinically significant prostate cancer detection, route differences are less consistent. PREVENT reported similar clinically significant cancer detection: 53% transperineal vs 50% transrectal. A 2024 prospective-study meta-analysis found no significant difference in clinically significant cancer detection between MRI-targeted TP and TR biopsy.

TRANSLATE, a large UK randomized trial, found higher detection of Gleason Grade Group ≥2 cancer with local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy than transrectal biopsy. Earlier meta-analysis and some observational MRI-fusion studies suggested transperineal biopsy may better detect anterior prostate clinically significant cancer.

Interpretation: both routes can diagnose clinically significant cancer. Transperineal may be advantageous for anterior/apical access and in some modern pathways, but local technique, MRI quality, targeting, and sampling plan matter.

docs.title: Pain, tolerability, and anesthesia
docs.text:
Historically, transperineal biopsy was associated with template mapping and general/spinal anesthesia. Modern local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy is feasible. A systematic review of local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy found low procedural abandonment and low infection-related complications, with greatest pain during anesthetic administration.

PREVENT found slightly worse periprocedural pain with transperineal biopsy, but the effect was small and resolved by 7 days. TRANSLATE reported more immediate pain and embarrassment with local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy than transrectal biopsy, while longer-term urinary and sexual function outcomes were not meaningfully different.

Interpretation: transperineal biopsy is no longer necessarily an operating-room procedure, but patients should ask exactly how anesthesia and positioning are handled.

docs.title: Antibiotic stewardship
docs.text:
Transrectal biopsy generally requires antibiotic prophylaxis; in higher-risk settings, rectal swab-targeted antibiotics, augmented prophylaxis, and rectal povidone-iodine preparation may be used. Transperineal biopsy can often be performed with no or reduced antibiotics in low-risk patients, depending on local protocol and patient risk factors.

Interpretation: antibiotic stewardship is one of the strongest system-level reasons for the shift toward transperineal biopsy.

docs.title: EAU Guidelines — Diagnostic Evaluation: prostate biopsy route and infection prevention
docs.text:
• Source: European Association of Urology prostate cancer guideline, diagnostic evaluation chapter.
  • URL: https://uroweb.org/guidelines/prostate-cancer/chapter/diagnostic-evaluation
  • Category: Guidelines & Care Pathways; Diagnostic Workup
  • Evidence type: Clinical guideline synthesis.
  • Patient relevance: Provides current guideline direction and infection-prevention recommendations.
  • Main finding: EAU recommends performing prostate biopsy using the transperineal approach due to low infectious complication risk and better antibiotic stewardship; it also recommends rectal povidone-iodine preparation and targeted/augmented prophylaxis for transrectal biopsy.
  • Limitations: Guidelines synthesize population-level evidence; local expertise and patient factors still matter.
  • Questions for clinician: Does this centre follow a transperineal-first approach, and if not, what infection-prevention protocol is used for transrectal biopsy?

docs.title: PREVENT randomized trial — transperineal without antibiotics vs transrectal with targeted prophylaxis
docs.text:
• Citation: Hu JC, et al. European Urology. 2024; also summarized in JAMA Oncology 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.eururo.2023.12.015; PMID 38212178. JAMA Oncology PMID 39298143.
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38212178/
  • Evidence type: Multicenter randomized trial.
  • Population: 658 biopsy-naïve participants randomized to transperineal biopsy without antibiotic prophylaxis vs transrectal biopsy with rectal culture-targeted prophylaxis.
  • Main finding: 0 infections in transperineal vs 4 infections (1.4%) in transrectal; clinically significant cancer detection was similar (53% vs 50%). Transperineal pain was slightly higher during the procedure but resolved by 7 days.
  • Patient relevance: Strong modern evidence that office transperineal biopsy can avoid antibiotics without compromising detection.
  • Limitations: Transrectal comparator used optimized targeted prophylaxis; results may differ where prophylaxis is less rigorous.
  • Questions for clinician: If transrectal biopsy is offered, is rectal-swab-targeted prophylaxis used?

docs.title: ProBE-PC randomized clinical trial — complications after transrectal and transperineal biopsy
docs.text:
• Citation: Journal of Urology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000003788; PMID 37976319
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37976319/
  • Evidence type: Prospective pragmatic randomized clinical trial.
  • Population: 718 men who underwent office transrectal or transperineal prostate biopsy under local anesthesia.
  • Main finding: Composite infectious complications were similar: 2.6% transrectal and 2.7% transperineal; no sepsis occurred in either group; noninfectious complications were also similar.
  • Patient relevance: Shows both approaches can be clinically viable and safe in controlled office settings.
  • Limitations: Composite endpoint included relatively broad infection-related events; local protocols matter.
  • Questions for clinician: What outcomes does this practice track—sepsis, hospitalization, antibiotics, fever, urinary retention, bleeding?

docs.title: TRANSLATE randomized trial — local-anesthetic transperineal vs transrectal biopsy
docs.text:
• Citation: Lancet Oncology. 2025.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00100-7; PMID 40139210
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40139210/
  • Evidence type: Multicenter randomized controlled trial.
  • Population: 1,126 biopsy-naïve UK participants investigated for possible prostate cancer after pre-biopsy MRI.
  • Main finding: Gleason Grade Group ≥2 cancer was detected in 60% assigned to local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy vs 54% assigned to transrectal biopsy. Hospital admission for infection was <1% in LATP vs 2% in TRUS. Immediate pain/embarrassment was more common with LATP.
  • Patient relevance: Large modern RCT directly comparing local-anesthetic transperineal and transrectal biopsy.
  • Limitations: UK pathway; mostly White British participants; excludes some high-risk infection/anatomy groups.
  • Questions for clinician: Does local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy improve sampling for my MRI lesion, and how is comfort managed?

docs.title: Transperineal versus transrectal MRI-targeted prostate biopsy — systematic review and meta-analysis of prospective studies
docs.text:
• Citation: European Urology Oncology. 2024.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.euo.2024.07.009; PMID 39095298
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39095298/
  • Evidence type: Systematic review/meta-analysis of prospective randomized trials.
  • Population: PREVENT, ProBE-PC, and PERFECT trials.
  • Main finding: No significant difference in clinically significant cancer detection, infection, sepsis, urinary retention, or pain by 7 days; transperineal biopsy can omit antibiotics without increasing infection risk.
  • Patient relevance: Balanced synthesis of the newest prospective evidence.
  • Limitations: Only three RCTs; heterogeneous protocols and pain reporting.
  • Questions for clinician: Is the main advantage in my case infection risk, lesion access, antibiotic avoidance, or all three?

docs.title: Local-anesthetic transperineal prostate biopsy — systematic review
docs.text:
• Citation: BJU International. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1111/bju.15906; PMID 36177521
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36177521/
  • Evidence type: Systematic review.
  • Population: 35 publications, 113,944 men undergoing local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy.
  • Main finding: Local-anesthetic transperineal biopsy had a low infection-related complication rate, low procedural abandonment, and viable diagnostic performance; greatest pain scores were during local anesthetic administration.
  • Patient relevance: Addresses the practical question: “Can TP biopsy be done without general anesthesia?”
  • Limitations: High heterogeneity and publication bias; limited formal cost analyses.
  • Questions for clinician: Is this performed under local anesthetic, sedation, or general anesthesia at this centre?

docs.title: Transperineal MRI-targeted biopsy may perform better for anterior clinically significant cancer
docs.text:
• Citation: Clinical Genitourinary Cancer. 2019.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.clgc.2019.05.006; PMID 31281065
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31281065/
  • Evidence type: Systematic review/meta-analysis.
  • Population: Men with positive mpMRI undergoing TP or TR MRI-targeted biopsy.
  • Main finding: TP MRI-targeted biopsy detected more clinically significant cancer in the included studies, especially anterior-zone lesions.
  • Patient relevance: Lesion location may influence route choice.
  • Limitations: Older evidence, smaller datasets, nonrandomized comparisons; newer RCTs are more mixed.
  • Questions for clinician: Is my MRI lesion anterior/apical or otherwise difficult to sample transrectally?

docs.title: Prostate cancer detection and complications of TP vs TR MRI-fusion guided biopsies
docs.text:
• Citation: Urology. 2023.
  • DOI/PMID: DOI 10.1016/j.urology.2023.04.003; PMID 37059232
  • URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37059232/
  • Evidence type: Retrospective MRI-fusion biopsy comparison.
  • Population: 361 patients undergoing TP or TR MRI-targeted biopsy with concurrent systematic biopsy.
  • Main finding: Clinically significant cancer detection and complication rates did not differ significantly by route.
  • Patient relevance: Supports the idea that both routes can work well when MRI-targeting and systematic sampling are performed properly.
  • Limitations: Retrospective, single system/time period, possible selection bias.
  • Questions for clinician: Is operator experience more important than route in this particular practice?

docs.title: Clinician discussion checklist
docs.text:
• Is biopsy needed now, or should PSA/MRI/risk calculation be repeated first?
  • If biopsy is needed, why transperineal or why transrectal in my case?
  • What is the centre’s infection/sepsis/hospitalization rate for each route?
  • Will the biopsy be MRI-targeted, systematic, or both?
  • Where is the MRI lesion: anterior, apical, posterior, transition zone, or peripheral zone?
  • What anesthesia is used: local anesthetic, sedation, spinal/general anesthesia?
  • What pain, bleeding, urinary retention, infection, and sexual/urinary side-effect rates should I expect?
  • If transrectal, will there be rectal swab-targeted antibiotics, augmented prophylaxis, and/or povidone-iodine rectal preparation?
  • If transperineal, are antibiotics omitted or used, and what infection-risk factors would change that?
  • How are anticoagulants/antiplatelets managed?
  • How many cores will be taken and how will samples be labeled for pathology?
  • What symptoms after biopsy require urgent contact?

docs.title: Repository interpretation
docs.text:
This evidence strengthens the Diagnostic Workup category by adding biopsy-route decision context:

  1. Transperineal is increasingly favored because it avoids rectal mucosa, reduces infection concern, and improves antibiotic stewardship.
  2. Transrectal remains viable where local expertise is strong and infection-prevention protocols are rigorous.
  3. Cancer detection is broadly similar in many modern studies, though transperineal may help with anterior/apical sampling and TRANSLATE found higher Grade Group ≥2 detection.
  4. The best route is contextual: MRI lesion location, patient anatomy, infection risk, anticoagulation, anesthesia tolerance, local equipment, and operator experience all matter.

docs.title: Medical Safety Policy
docs.text: This repository is educational and research-focused. It must not provide personal medical advice.

docs.title: Required safety language
docs.text:
For diagnosis, medications, surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, procedures, supplements, or screening changes, include:

  • Not medical advice
  • Discuss with a qualified clinician
  • Possible contraindications, harms, and trade-offs
  • Monitoring considerations
  • Uncertainty level

docs.title: Prohibited automated recommendations
docs.text:
Hermes should not directly recommend that the user:

  • Start, stop, or change treatment or medication
  • Delay urgent medical care or ignore symptoms
  • Choose one treatment option without clinician review
  • Treat screening, diagnostic, or risk-stratification summaries as a diagnosis

docs.title: Preferred framing
docs.text:
Use:

  • “Worth discussing with a clinician”
  • “Evidence suggests, but applicability depends on risk group…”
  • “Potential harms include…”
  • “This changes the evidence map by…”

Avoid:

  • “You should choose…”
  • “This proves…”
  • “This rules out cancer…”

docs.title: Include by default
docs.text:
• New or updated guidelines from NCCN, EAU, AUA, ASCO, NICE, USPSTF, Cancer Australia, or major cancer centers
  • Randomized trials, meta-analyses, and large prospective studies relevant to prostate cancer diagnosis, treatment, surveillance, or outcomes
  • Diagnostic accuracy studies with clear clinical endpoints
  • Patient-reported outcome studies for urinary, sexual, bowel, fatigue, mental health, or survivorship outcomes
  • Safety updates that may affect clinical conversations

docs.title: Exclude or down-rank
docs.text:
• Preclinical-only studies unless clearly practice-relevant
  • Small retrospective single-center studies without a patient-facing implication
  • Press releases without accessible study details
  • Opinion pieces not tied to evidence or guideline changes
  • Commercial claims without independent validation

docs.title: Scoring rubric
docs.text:
Score 0–10. Include items scoring >= 7.5 or items with lower score only if they are important safety/guideline watchlist items.

Dimension Points Evidence quality 0–3 Patient decision relevance 0–2 Recency/novelty 0–1 Applicability to real clinical pathways 0–2 Clarity of risks/limitations 0–1 Source credibility 0–1

docs.title: Required note format
docs.text:
• Citation/title/authors/date/source URL
  • Evidence type and population
  • Category/categories
  • Summary
  • Main finding
  • Patient relevance
  • Limitations
  • Safety/clinical cautions
  • Whether it changes a category page or decision map

docs.title: Patient-Perspective Overview
docs.text: This section turns the evidence library into a patient-oriented decision map.

docs.title: Typical journey
docs.text:
1. Screening or suspicion — PSA, DRE, family history, urinary symptoms, incidental finding
  2. Risk clarification — repeat PSA, PSA density, MRI, risk calculators
  3. Diagnosis — biopsy approach, pathology, Grade Group/Gleason, tumor volume
  4. Staging and aggressiveness — imaging, clinical stage, genomic classifiers if appropriate
  5. Management choice — active surveillance, surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, clinical trial, or palliative focus depending on risk/stage
  6. Follow-up — surveillance, PSA monitoring, side-effect management, recurrence detection

docs.title: Repository rule
docs.text: Summaries should help prepare for a clinician discussion, not substitute for it.

docs.title: Diagnosis and risk
docs.text:
• What is the most likely explanation for my PSA/test result?
  • Should PSA be repeated before further testing?
  • Would mpMRI help decide whether biopsy is needed?
  • If biopsy is recommended, why this approach and what are the risks?
  • If cancer is found, what Grade Group, stage, and risk category is it?

docs.title: Treatment choice
docs.text:
• Is active surveillance reasonable for this risk profile?
  • What are the expected outcomes with surgery vs radiation vs surveillance?
  • What side effects are most likely for someone in my situation?
  • How many similar cases do you manage per year?
  • Should I get a second opinion or multidisciplinary review?

docs.title: Follow-up
docs.text:
• What result would trigger a change in plan?
  • How often would PSA, MRI, biopsy, or imaging be repeated?
  • How will quality-of-life side effects be monitored and treated?

docs.title: A. Before diagnosis
docs.text:
• What prompted testing?
  • What is the PSA trend, PSA density, and age-adjusted context?
  • Is MRI appropriate before biopsy?
  • What are the false-positive, false-negative, and overdiagnosis issues?

docs.title: B. If biopsy is being considered
docs.text:
• Transperineal vs transrectal approach
  • MRI-targeted plus systematic biopsy vs systematic alone
  • Infection, bleeding, urinary retention, and pain risks
  • Whether antibiotics or anesthesia/sedation are relevant

docs.title: C. If cancer is found
docs.text:
• Grade Group/Gleason score
  • Number and percentage of positive cores
  • PSA, PSA density, MRI lesion size, clinical stage
  • Whether genomic classifier or PSMA PET/CT is relevant

docs.title: D. Management options
docs.text:
• Active surveillance
  • Radical prostatectomy
  • External beam radiation therapy
  • Brachytherapy
  • Focal therapy/investigational approaches
  • ADT or systemic therapy for higher-risk/advanced disease

docs.title: E. Outcomes to track
docs.text:
• Cancer control and metastasis-free survival
  • Need for later treatment
  • Urinary incontinence/irritative symptoms
  • Sexual function
  • Bowel symptoms
  • Fatigue, mood, and quality of life

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• PSA: Prostate-specific antigen, a blood marker that can rise for cancer and non-cancer reasons.
  • PSA density: PSA divided by prostate volume; can help interpret PSA in context.
  • mpMRI: Multiparametric MRI, used to identify suspicious prostate lesions.
  • PI-RADS: MRI scoring system for likelihood of clinically significant cancer.
  • Biopsy: Tissue sampling to diagnose cancer and determine grade.
  • Gleason score / Grade Group: Pathology grading of cancer aggressiveness.
  • Active surveillance: Structured monitoring for lower-risk cancer, with treatment if risk changes.
  • Radical prostatectomy: Surgical removal of the prostate.
  • Radiation therapy: Treatment using targeted radiation, including external beam or brachytherapy.
  • ADT: Androgen deprivation therapy, systemic treatment that lowers androgen signaling.
  • PSMA PET: Imaging test that can detect prostate cancer spread or recurrence in selected contexts.
  • mCRPC: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, meaning metastatic disease that is progressing despite castrate-level androgen suppression.
  • MSI-H/dMMR: Microsatellite instability-high or mismatch-repair deficient tumor biology; uncommon in prostate cancer but can make immune checkpoint therapy more relevant.
  • Sipuleucel-T: An autologous cellular immunotherapy with randomized survival evidence in selected asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC.
  • Immune checkpoint inhibitor: A drug such as pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab, or ipilimumab that alters immune braking signals; benefits in prostate cancer are usually biomarker-selected or investigational.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
• Which diagnosis-first pathway best reduces unnecessary biopsy while avoiding missed significant cancers?
  • Which patients benefit most from MRI-targeted biopsy, systematic biopsy, or both?
  • How should genomic classifiers change decisions for borderline risk groups?
  • Which active surveillance schedules optimize safety while minimizing anxiety and procedures?
  • Which treatment choices best preserve urinary, sexual, and bowel quality of life for comparable cancer-control outcomes?
